Canonical Wnt signaling enhances pro-inflammatory response to titanium by macrophages
Derek Avery, Lais Morandini, Luke S. Sheakley, Arth H. Shah, Loc Bui, Jefferson O. Abaricia, Rene Olivares-Navarrete *
Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Engineering, Virginia Commonwealth University, Richmond, VA, United States
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords: Macrophage Wnt signaling Microstructured titanium Wnt3a
1. Introduction

Biomaterials for clinical use can be fabricated from a wide array of materials and using a variety manufacturing processes to enhance their performance in the body. The primary goals following implantation of bone-dwelling biomaterials are resolution of wound healing and ultimately  tissue  integration,  and  implant  success  is  determined  by  the initial  inflammatory  response  and  the  rate  of  synthesis  of  functional tissue  that  surrounds  the  implant  [1 -3].  The  implantation procedure causes damage to cells and tissues that release damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) into the implant site that attract leukocytes, particularly innate immune cells like neutrophils and macrophages, to the site of injury.

Macrophages are significant in the immune response to biomaterials, modulating  inflammation  via  the  regulation  of  cytokines  and  eliminating pathogens and cellular debris via phagocytosis. Macrophages are highly plastic, myeloid-derived cells that are sensitive to changes in the
A B S T R A C T

Biomaterial characteristics like surface roughness and wettability can determine the phenotype of macrophages following implantation. We have demonstrated that inhibiting Wnt ligand secretion abolishes macrophage polarization in vitro and in vivo ; however, the role of canonical Wnt signaling in macrophage activation in response to physical and chemical biomaterial cues is unknown. The aim of this study was to understand whether canonical Wnt signaling affects the response of macrophages to titanium (Ti) surface roughness or wettability in vitro and in vivo . Activating canonical Wnt signaling increased expression of toll-like receptors and interleukin receptors and secreted pro-inflammatory cytokines and reduced anti-inflammatory cytokines on Ti, regardless of surface properties. Inhibiting canonical Wnt signaling reduced pro-inflammatory cytokines on all Ti surfaces and increased  anti-inflammatory  cytokines  on  rough  or  rough-hydrophilic  Ti. In  vivo ,  activating  canonical  Wnt signaling  increased  total  macrophages,  pro-inflammatory  macrophages,  and  T  cells  and  decreased  antiinflammatory macrophages on both smooth and rough-hydrophilic implants. Functionally, canonical Wnt activation increases pro-inflammatory macrophage response to cell and cell-extracellular matrix lysates. These results demonstrate that activating canonical Wnt signaling primes macrophages to a pro-inflammatory phenotype that affects their response to Ti implants in vitro and in vivo .

inflammatory microenvironment [4,5]. Macrophages differentiate from circulating  monocytes  into  pro-inflammatory  or  anti-inflammatory phenotypes, depending on the biochemical and mechanical cues at the injury site [6,7], and recruit other immune cells and progenitor cells to the injury site to facilitate wound healing and tissue regeneration.

Activation of immune cells can be directed by the surface properties of  implanted  biomaterials.  Our  group  has  demonstrated  that  macrophages differentiate towards an anti-inflammatory M2-like phenotype in response  to  both  rough  and  hydrophilic  titanium  surfaces  (Ti)  as compared  to  smooth  surfaces,  both in  vivo and in vitro [2,6 -8]. Furthermore, our group has also found that the same surface characteristics promote osteoblastic differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), accelerating osseointegration [9].

The Wingless/Integrase-1 (Wnt) signaling pathway is critical during bone regeneration and osseointegration of biomaterial implants [10 -12]. Wnt ligands are lipid-modified glycoproteins that bind to cell surface Frizzled (FZD) receptors that transmit the biological signal to D. Avery et al.

dishevelled-1, -2, or -3 [13 -15]. Mammals have 19 distinct Wnt ligands that  activate  one  of  three  Wnt  signaling  pathways:  Wntbeta -catenin dependent pathway, also called the canonical Wnt pathway, and two beta -catenin independent pathways, Wnt planar cell polarity pathway and Wnt-calcium dependent pathway [10,16 -19]. The hallmark of canonical Wnt signaling is the cytoplasmic accumulation and nuclear translocation of beta -catenin. Without canonical Wnt ligands, cytoplasmic beta -catenin is degraded by the beta -catenin destruction complex, which includes axin 2, adenomatosis  polyposis  coli  (APC)  protein  phosphatase  2A  (PP2A), glycogen synthase kinase 3 (GSK3), and casein kinase 1 alpha (CK1 alpha ) [17, 20]. Phosphorylation of beta -catenin by CK1 alpha or GSK3 results in beta -catenin ubiquitination  and  proteolytic  destruction  [21 -24].  Canonical  Wnt ligand  recognition  by  the  co-receptor  frizzled  (FZD)  and  lipoprotein receptor-related protein (LRP) 5/6 cause accumulation of beta -catenin and eventually translocation into the nucleus where it acts as a coactivator of transcription factors T-cell factor (TCF) and lymphoid enhancer-binding factor  (LEF)  family  [25 -28]. Many prior studies have examined Wnt signaling  in  cancer,  the  tumor  microenvironment,  and  other  malignancies, but Wnt signaling also plays an important role in the regulation of bone regeneration and functionality [11]. Absence or inhibition of Wnt signaling leads to loss of bone regeneration and mass, inability of osteoblasts  to  differentiate  and  proliferate,  and  degradation  of  the beta -catenin protein in the cytoplasm via the beta -catenin destruction complex [11,12,21].

Wnt signaling can also modulate immune cell function, affecting cell development, activation, and immunomodulation [9,29,30]. In macrophages, Wnt signaling during tissue healing has been associated with a stronger pro-inflammatory microenvironment and fibrotic response [9, 10]. We have previously shown that macrophages upregulate expression of  Wnt  genes  in  response  to  surface  characteristics  on  Ti  [9,30]. Furthermore, we have shown that Wnt ligands are needed for immune cell  recruitment  and  activation  and  stem  cell  recruitment  following biomaterial implantation and for tissue healing using a macrophage-specific Wntless ( Wls )  knockout mouse model [9]. However, the effect of specific Wnt ligands or Wnt signaling pathways on macrophages during tissue healing after biomaterial implantation remains unknown.

The aim of this study was to determine the role of canonical Wnt signaling in macrophage polarization in response to changes in surface topography and hydrophilicity of Ti implants during bone regeneration and implant integration. We hypothesized that activation of the Wnt/ beta -catenin  signaling  pathway  is  needed  for  pro-inflammatory  macrophage polarization in response to modified titanium surfaces.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Titanium disks, implants, and material characterization

Smooth, rough, and rough-hydrophilic Ti disks and implants were fabricated by Institut Straumann AG (Basel, Switzerland) as previously reported [1,2,31 -36]. Briefly, 15 mm disks were created from 1 mm thick unalloyed titanium grade 2. Disks were degreased in ultrasound acetone bath and then immersed in a solution of 2% ammonium fluoride, 2% hydrofluoric acid, 10% nitric acid to produce the smooth Ti disks. Smooth disks were sand-blasted with 0.25 -0.50 mm coarse-grit corundum  and  then  acid  etched  in  a  mixture  of  HCl  and  H2SO4 to create  rough  Ti  disks.  Smooth  and  rough  Ti  disks  were  rinsed  in deionized  water  and  dried  after  processing.  Rough-hydrophilic  disks were fabricated in the same way as rough Ti disks but rinsed under nitrogen  atmosphere after acid etching and stored  in an isotonic NaCl solution to prevent exposure to atmospheric air. Titanium implants (4 mm length) were fabricated from 1 mm diameter rods and modified following the same protocols as disks. Disks and implants were sterilized by gamma -irradiation before use.

Surface roughness was assessed qualitatively using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and quantitatively using confocal microscopy (LSM 910 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20 x objective and a total measurement area of 798 u m x 798 u m. The arithmetic mean height of the scale limited surface (Sa) was calculated using a moving average Gaussian filter with a cut-off wavelength of 30 u m. Mean surface roughness was measured at six different areas of three separate samples for each surface type. Surface hydrophilicity was assessed by sessile drop contact angle using a Ram   e-Hart goniometer (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). Measurements using 1 u l drops of deionized water were performed at three locations per sample. Oxide layer composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a PhI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) Spectra were acquired at a base pressure of 1 x 10 7 Pa using a focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 u m. The instrument was run in the FAT analyzer mode. Pass energy used for survey scans was 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV for detail spectra. Data were analyzed using the program CasaXPS. The signals were integrated following Shirley background subtraction.  Measurements were performed at three separate areas on each sample.
2.2. Macrophage isolation

Primary murine macrophages were differentiated from bone marrow harvested from 12-week-old male C57BL/6J mice (The Jackson Laboratory, Bar Harbor, ME) under the approval of the Virginia Commonwealth  University  Institutional  Animal  Care  and  Use  Committee (Protocol 10001108 CE). Mice were euthanized by CO2  asphyxiation and femur bones were removed. The bone marrow was flushed with PBS and treated with red cell lysis buffer (ACK Buffer, ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA). Nave macrophages were derived by culturing cells in Dulbecco ' s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, ThermoFisher) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 u g/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher), and 30 ng/mL macrophage colony stimulating factor (M-CSF, BioLegend, San Diego, CA) for 7 days at 37  C, 5% CO2, and 100% humidity. Supplemented media was refreshed on day 4 of culture. On day 7, macrophages were detached with Accutase (Innovative Cell Technologies, San Diego, CA) for use in subsequent experiments.
2.3. Canonical Wnt signaling and Tlr4 gene expression time course

Nave macrophages were seeded at a density of 50,000 cells/cm 2 on TCPS or smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic Ti disks (n = 6/variable) and cultured for 3, 6, 12, 24, or 36 h in DMEM (ThermoFisher) supplemented  with  10%  fetal  bovine  serum  (ThermoFisher),  50  U/mL penicillin-50 u g/mL  streptomycin  (ThermoFisher).  After  incubation, mRNA  was  extracted  as  described  below  and  expression  of Wnt3a , Ctnnb1 , Lrp5 , Tlr4, and Rps 18 were measured by qPCR.
2.4. Effect of Canonical Wnt signaling on macrophage activation

To  understand  the  significance  of  canonical  Wnt  signaling  on macrophage activation, nave macrophages were plated on tissue culture polystyrene (TCPS) and treated with either activators (recombinant mouse Wnt3a (5, 10, 15, 20, or 25 ng/mL, R & D Systems, Minneapolis, MN), AZD 2858 (0.1, 1, 2.5, or 5 u g/mL, Cayman Chemical, Ann Arbor, MI), or SB 216763 (8, 16, 24, 32 u g/mL, Cayman Chemical) or an inhibitor (PNU 74654, 1, 2, 3, or 4 u g/mL, Cayman Chemical) of canonical Wnt signaling. Small molecules were resuspended in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) before use and DMSO was used as control. We selected Wnt3a because in our previous studies, Wnt3a was one of the earliest canonical Wnt ligands expressed by macrophages in response to Ti implants and also  had  a  strong  pro-inflammatory  effect  on  macrophages  [9].  AZD 2858 and SB 216763 are potent selective inhibitors of GSK3 that induce cytoplasmic accumulation of beta -catenin and beta -catenin-dependent gene expression [37 -40]. PNU 74654 disrupts canonical Wnt signaling by D. Avery et al.

inhibiting the interaction of beta -catenin with TCF4 [41 -43]. Macrophages were treated for 12 h for mRNA analysis or 24 h for protein analysis in conditioned media as described below and compared to vehicle-treated controls (n = 6 independent cultures/variable).
2.5. Effect of Canonical Wnt signaling on macrophage activation cultured on Ti modified surfaces

To understand the effect of canonical Wnt signaling activation or inhibition on macrophage activation and inflammatory microenvironment,  nave  macrophages  were  seeded  on  TCPS,  smooth,  rough,  or rough-hydrophilic Ti disks at 50,000 cells/cm 2 and treated with activators (15 ng/mL Wnt3a, 1 u g/mL AZD 2858, or 3 u g/mL PNU 74654) or an inhibitor (16 u g/mL SB 216763) of canonical Wnt signaling. Macrophages  were  cultured  for  12  h  for  mRNA  analysis  or  for  24  h  for protein analysis in conditioned media as described below and compared to untreated controls (n = 6 independent cultures/variable).
2.6. mRNA expression

mRNA was extracted from cells using TRIzol, and 1 u g of RNA was converted to cDNA using iScript cDNA synthesis kit (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA).  qPCR  was  performed  using  SsoAdvanced  Universal  SYBR  green supermix (Bio-Rad) to assess mRNA expression of interleukin receptors, Toll-like  receptors  (TLR),  and  genes  associated  with  canonical  Wnt signaling using commercially-available primers (Bio-Rad PrimePCR TM , Supplemental Table 1). Differences were determined by 2 DeltaDelta CT analysis calculated using endogenous housekeeping gene (Rsp18) and respective controls.
2.7. Conditioned media protein analysis

Conditioned media was collected and stored at 80  C until analysis. Levels of IL-1 beta , IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, and Tnfalpha were measured by enzymelinked  immunosorbent  assay  (ELISA,  BioLegend,  San  Diego,  CA) following manufacturer ' s instructions. Levels of secreted proteins were normalized to DNA content measured in cell lysate (Quant-IT TM PicoGreen dsDNA Assay, Thermo Fisher).
2.8. Lrp5 silencing in RAW264.7 macrophage-like cells

RAW 264.7 cells (American Type Culture Collection, Manassas, VA), a  macrophage-like  cell  line,  was  used  to  silence  the  canonical  Wnt signaling  co-receptor  LDL  Receptor  Related  Protein  5  ( Lrp5 ).  RAW 264.7 cells were transduced with shRNA lentiviral transduction particles (Mission (R) , Millipore Sigma, St. Louis, MO) targeting Lrp5 (TRCN0000109325). TurboGFP TM (GFP) transduction particles (Millipore Sigma, St. Louis, MO) were used as control. RAW 264.7 cells were plated at 50,000 cells/cm 2 in RPMI 1640 media (ThermoFisher) with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher), 1% sodium pyruvate (ThermoFisher),  and  1%  penicillin/streptomycin  (ThermoFisher).  Particles were added to the cells at a multiplicity of infection of 5. After 24 h incubation, transduced cells were selected with 0.75 u g/mL of puromycin (ThermoFisher) for 1 week. Lrp5 silencing of cells (sh Lrp5 ) was confirmed using real-time qPCR with commercially-available primers (Bio-Rad, Supplemental Table 1), and GFP positive cells were confirmed by flow cytometry.
2.9. Effect of Lrp5 silencing on RAW 264.7 cells response to modified Ti surfaces

To understand the effect of silencing the canonical Wnt co-receptor Lrp5 in macrophage-like cell response to modified Ti surfaces, sh Lrp5 or GFP + RAW 264.7 cells were plated on TCPS or smooth, rough, roughhydrophilic  Ti  surfaces  at  a  density  of  50,000  cells/cm 2 .  Cells  were incubated for 24 h and after incubation conditioned media was collected for cytokine analysis as described previously.
2.10. Effect of Canonical Wnt signaling modulation on Macrophages and T cells in vivo

Twelve-week-old male C57BL/6 J mice were injected with activators (AZD2858 (30 mg/kg, intraperitoneally [i.p.]) or SB 216763 (5 mg/kg, i.  p.)  or  an  inhibitor  (PNU 74654 (1 mg/kg, i. p.)) of canonical Wnt signaling (n = 7 mice/variable). Concentrations were selected based on activation  or  inhibition  of  canonical  Wnt  signaling  as  previously  reported [44 -46]. Small molecules were dissolved in PBS containing 5% DMSO, 30% PEG300, and 1% Tween-80 (vehicle control) and injected daily for 3 days. After 3 days of injections with activators or inhibitor, mice were euthanized by CO2  asphyxiation and circulating blood and femoral  bone  marrow  were  harvested  for  macrophage  and  T  cell immunophenotyping  as  described  below  and  compared  to  vehicle controls.
2.11. Effect of Canonical Wnt signaling on macrophage response to femoral implants in vivo

Twelve-week-old male C57BL/6J mice were anesthetized using isoflurane delivered in O2 gas and monitored for unconsciousness by pedal reflex. The skin overlaying the knee was opened and the patellar tendon was bisected longitudinally using a scalpel and retracted to reveal the femoral condyles. A surgical dental bur was used to access the medullary canal and a cylindrical Ti implant (  = 1 mm) with a smooth or roughhydrophilic (rough-hydro) surface was then press fit (n = 6 mice per implant condition) and the implant placement confirmed by x-ray. Injections  with  canonical  Wnt  activators  (AZD2858,  30  mg/kg;  SB 216763, 5 mg/kg; or PNU 74654, 1 mg/kg) or vehicle control were administrated daily from one day prior surgical procedure to day 2 postimplantation (day 3 time point) or until day 5 post-implantation (day 7 time point). Mice were administered 1 mg/kg buprenorphine SR LAB prior to recovery from anesthesia to relieve post-operative pain for 72 h. Animals were monitored until initial ambulation and every 24 h afterwards.  All  animals  had  access  to  food  and  water ad  libitum for  the duration of the study. No signs of infection were seen in this study.

On post-operative day 3 or day 7, mice were euthanized by CO2 asphyxiation. Blood was collected by cardiac puncture and stored in a 1.5 mL tube with 0.5 M EDTA to prevent clotting. Blood was centrifuged at 1500 g for 15 min. After centrifugation, plasma was transferred into a new 1.5 mL tube and stored at 80  C for analysis.  To isolate  periimplant tissue,  femurs  were cut  mid  shaft,  and  the  implant  and  surrounding marrow were flushed using Accutase. Following a 20-min incubation in Accutase at 37  C, cells were washed with staining buffer (BioLegend) before staining for flow cytometry analysis. A second set of mice were used to analyze the cytokine microenvironment in the periimplant  tissue.  To  isolate  peri-implant  tissue,  femurs  were  cut  mid shaft, and the implant and surrounding marrow were flushed with 500 u L chilled PBS without Ca ++ or Mg ++ . Samples were vortexed for 2 min and centrifuged at 500 g for 10 min. Supernatants were transferred into 1.5 mL tubes and stored at 80  C. Plasma and supernatants from periimplant tissues were thawed and levels of IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-10, IL-12p40, IL-23, CCL2, TNFalpha , and TGFbeta 1 were measured using a custom Multiplex ELISA (LEGENDplex, BioLegend, San Diego, CA) following manufacturer ' s instructions.
2.12. Flow cytometry

Single cell suspensions from blood or bone marrow were used for flow cytometry analysis. Prior to fluorescent staining, red blood cells were lysed with ACK lysis buffer (ThermoFisher) and Fc receptors were blocked by incubation with TruStain FcX (anti-CD16/32, BioLegend) to prevent non-specific binding of subsequent fluorescent antibodies. Cell suspensions were then incubated with fluorescent antibodies to identify D. Avery et al.

macrophages  (CD45 + /CD11b + /MHCII + /F4/80 + ),  pro-inflammatory macrophages (Mac + /CD80 + ), anti-inflammatory macrophages (Mac + /CD206 + ), T cells (CD45 + /CD3 + ), CD4 + T cells (CD3 + /CD4 + ), and CD8 + T cells (CD3 + /CD8 + ) (BioLegend) (Supplemental Table 2). 2.13. Effect of Wnt signaling on macrophage response in DAMP challenge assay

Cell lysates were generated by seeding mouse fibroblasts (CRL-2645, ATCC, Manassas, VA) a density of 20,000 cells/cm 2 in 6 well plates and culturing in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 50 U/mL  penicillin-50 u g/mL  streptomycin  (ThermoFisher)  for  7  days. After incubation, cells were washed 3 times in warm PBS and then cells were sonicated for 1 min in 1 mL of fresh PBS. To produce extracellular matrix  (ECM) + cell  lysates,  fibroblasts  were  cultured  for  7  days  in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, 50U/mL penicillin50 u g/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher), and 50 u g/mL L-ascorbic acid (Sigma-Aldrich,  St.  Louis,  MO).  After  incubation,  ECM + cells  were washed 3 times in warm PBS and then sonicated for 1 min in 1 mL of fresh PBS.

Nave  macrophages  were  seeded  on  TCPS  at  a  density  of  50,000 cells/cm 2 and treated with canonical Wnt activators (Wnt3a [15 ng/ mL], AZD 2858 [1 u g/mL], SB 216763 [4 u g/mL], or inhibitor (PNU 74654 [16 u g/mL]) for 12 h (n = 6/variable). DMSO was used as control. After incubation, macrophages were washed twice with warm PBS and treated with 5 mg/mL of cell lysate or ECM + cell lysate for 24 h. After incubation, levels of IL-1 beta , IL-6, and TNFalpha were measured in the conditioned media. Levels of cytokines were normalized by macrophage number.
2.14. Statistical analysis

Data are presented as mean +/- SD of n = 6 independent cultures per variable for in vitro experiments and n = 7 mice per variable for in vivo experiments. Statistical analysis was performed using Prism 8 (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA). Data were first subjected to the ShapiroWilk normality test. Results from this test indicated that the data were normally distributed. A one-factor, equal analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to test the null hypothesis that group means were equal at a significance level of alpha = 0.05, with post-hoc Tukey ' s HSD test for multiple comparisons.
3. Results
3.1. Titanium characterization

Higher surface roughness and increased topography was evident in SEM images on both rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti as result of the sand-blasting  and  acid  etching  as  compared  to  smooth  Ti  (Fig.  1A). Quantitatively, rough (Sa = 3.26 u m) and rough-hydrophilic (Sa = 3.24 u m) were significantly rougher than smooth Ti (Sa = 0.74 u m). We did not  identify  differences  in  surface  roughness  between  rough  Ti  and rough-hydrophilic  Ti  samples  either  qualitatively  or  quantitatively. Water contact angle measurements (Fig. 1B) showed that the most hydrophobic  surface  was  rough  Ti  ( theta = 110.5  )  while  the  roughhydrophilic Ti ( theta = 0  ) was superhydrophilic. Smooth Ti was normal ( theta = 63  ).  Wettability  of  our  Ti  samples  correlated  with  the  carbon content  of  the  oxide  layer.  Surface  chemical  composition  (Fig.  1C) showed that rough Ti, the most hydrophobic material tested, had the most C content in the oxide layer, while rough-hydrophilic Ti had the lowest C content in the oxide layer.
3.2. Wnt3a and beta -catenin expression Precede Lrp5 and Tlr4 upregulation

To  demonstrate  how  physicochemical  cues  alter  canonical  Wnt signaling on macrophages, we cultured nave macrophages on smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces, and TCPS as control. Wnt3a and Ctnnb1 expression by macrophages showed a time-dependent increase on all Ti surfaces and TCPS, with the highest expression observed in macrophages cultured on smooth Ti surfaces. 3.3. Canonical Wnt signaling alters macrophage activation in vitro

To  understand  the  significance  of  canonical  Wnt  signaling  on macrophage  activation,  macrophages  were  cultured  with  activators (Wnt3a, AZD 2858, and SB 216763) or an inhibitor (PNU 74654) of canonical Wnt signaling. Activation of canonical Wnt signaling lead to upregulation of canonical Wnt co-receptors Lrp5, Lrp6 (Fig. 3A) , Wnt canonical modulator Axin2 and toll-like receptors Tlr2, Tlr4, Tlr5, Tlr7, and Tlr9 (Fig. 4). In contrast, inhibiting canonical Wnt signaling with PNU 74654 decreased expression of Il6ra , Tlr1 , Tlr2 , Tlr4 , Tlr5 , Tlr7 , Tlr9 ,  and  increased  expression  of Il4ra and Il10ra (Figs.  3A  and  4). Secretion of inflammatory cytokines IL-1 beta ,  IL-6,  and  TNFalpha in conditioned media increased when macrophages were treated with canonical Wnt signaling activators (Wnt3a, AZD 2858 or SB 216763) (Fig. 3B). Furthermore, activation of canonical Wnt signaling resulted in a dose dependent decrease of IL-4 and IL-10 (Fig. 3B).
3.4. Activation of Canonical Wnt signaling upregulates pro-inflammatory expression in macrophages cultured on modified Ti surfaces

To demonstrate how canonical Wnt signaling affects the response of macrophages  to  Ti  surfaces,  macrophages  were  cultured  on  smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces and treated with activators or inhibitors of canonical Wnt signaling. Secretion of IL-1 beta , IL-6, and TNF was elevated on all Ti surfaces in comparison to TCPS control (Fig. 5). Activators of the Wnt/ beta -catenin pathway further enhanced the production of IL-1 beta , IL-6, and TNF on all Ti surfaces, particularly on smooth and rough Ti surfaces. Similarly, production of IL-4 was increased on all Ti surfaces  and  IL-10  production  was  heightened  on  rough  and  roughhydrophilic  surfaces  when  compared  to  control.  Conversely,  production of these cytokine proteins was upregulated with the pharmacological canonical Wnt signaling inhibitor, PNU 74654, specifically on rough and  rough-hydrophilic  Ti  surfaces.  However,  activators  of  canonical signaling  also  upregulated  cytokine  production  of  IL-4  and  IL-10  on rough hydrophilic surfaces. Pharmacological activators exhibited either a very small change, or no change in production of IL-4 and IL-10 on smooth and rough surfaces.

Macrophages altered expression canonical Wnt co-receptors ( Lrp5, Lrp6 ), interleukin receptors ( Il1r1, Il1rn, Il4ra, Il6ra, Il10ra ) and TLRs ( Tlr1,  Tlr2,  Tlr4,  Tlr5, and Tlr9 )  in  response  to  Ti  physicochemical characteristics. Smooth Ti surfaces upregulated the most Axin2 , Lrp5, Lrp6, Il1r1, Il6ra, Tlr1, Tlr2, Tlr4, Tlr5, and Tlr9 when compared to rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces (Fig. 6). In the other hand, roughhydrophilic  Ti  surfaces  upregulated  the  most Il1rn , Il4ra and Il10ra . Treatment with canonical Wnt signaling activators increased expression Axin2 , Lrp5, Lrp6, Il1r1, Il6ra, Tlr1, Tlr2, Tlr4, Tlr5, and Tlr9 in TCPS and all Ti surfaces. Furthermore, treatment with Wnt3a, AZD 2858 and SB 216763 decrease expression of Il1rn , Il4ra and Il10ra in all Ti surfaces when  compared  to  vehicle  treatment.  Inhibition  of  canonical  Wnt signaling with PNU 74654 decreased expression of Axin2, Lrp5, Lrp6, Il1r1, Il6ra, Tlr1, Tlr2, Tlr4, Tlr5, and Tlr9 in smooth and rough Ti surfaces  when  compared to  vehicle  group.  Additionally,  treatment  with PNU 74654 increased expression of Il1rn, Il4ra and Il10ra in TCPS and all Ti surfaces. Together these data suggests that activation of the canonical Wnt  pathway  increases  secretion  of  pro-inflammatory  cytokines  and interleukin receptor and TLR expression.
3.5. Lrp5 silencing reduces inflammatory cytokine release in response to modified Ti surfaces

We  aimed  to  investigate  the  effect  of  Wnt  canonical  signaling disruption by silencing Lrp5 in a macrophage-like cell line (RAW 264.7 cells). Lrp5 gene expression was significantly reduced in shLrp5 cells (75%)  when  compared  to  GFP-transduced  control  RAW  264.7  cells (Fig. 7). sh Lrp5 and GFP control RAW 264.7 cells responded to changes in  surface  roughness  and  hydrophilicity,  with  higher  levels  of  proinflammatory cytokines IL-1 beta ,  IL-6,  and  TNFalpha on smooth and rough Ti surfaces when compared to TCPS or rough-hydrophilic Ti. However, sh Lrp5 response to modified Ti surfaces was significantly lower when compared to GFP control RAW 264.7 cells (Fig. 7). Anti-inflammatory interleukins IL-4 and IL-10 were the highest on rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces and the lowest in smooth surfaces. Silencing of Lrp5 resulted in increased levels of IL-4 and IL-10 on all Ti surfaces, but the effect was more evident on smooth surfaces (Fig. 7).
3.6. Perturbation of Canonical Wnt signaling alters Macrophages and T cells in vivo

Mice administered canonical Wnt activators AZD 2858 or SB16763 increased proportion  of  macrophages  in  peripheral  blood,  but  only  AZD  2858 increased macrophages in the bone marrow. 3.7. Pharmacological activation of Canonical Wnt signaling alters local immune cell response to titanium physicochemical Properties and systemic inflammatory environment in vivo

To understand the effect of canonical Wnt signaling activation or inhibition on the local and systemic inflammatory response to Ti implants, we administered canonical Wnt activators (AZD 2858 and SB 216763) or an inhibitor (PNU 74654) to mice with implanted Ti implants. In vehicle treated mice, total macrophages and pro-inflammatory macrophages  were  higher  on  smooth  Ti  implants  in  comparison  to rough-hydrophilic Ti implants (Fig. 9). Similarly, higher proportions of T  cells  were  seen  on  smooth  implants  when  compared  to  roughhydrophilic.  Rough-hydrophilic  implants  showed  the  highest  antiinflammatory macrophages and the lowest T cell proportion.

When canonical Wnt signaling was activated with AZD 2858 or SB 216763, macrophages were elevated locally around smooth and roughhydrophilic Ti implants on day 3 post-implantation when compared to vehicle control or PNU 74654 (Fig. 9). Moreover, treatment with AZD 2858  or  SB  216763  increased  pro-inflammatory  macrophages  and reduced anti-inflammatory  macrophages  on  both  smooth  and  roughhydrophilic Ti implants when compared to vehicle or PNU 74654. Inhibition of canonical Wnt signaling with PNU 74654 slightly increased pro-inflammatory macrophages and reduced anti-inflammatory macrophages on rough-hydrophilic Ti implants when compared to the vehicle group. Treatment with PNU 74654 reduced T cells on both smooth and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants (Fig. 9). At day 7 post-implantation, all macrophages (total, pro- and anti-inflammatory) and T cells were lower in mice with rough-hydrophilic Ti implants when compared to smooth Ti implants. Pro-inflammatory macrophages were dramatically increased in mice with rough-hydrophilic implants when compared with vehicle treatment group. Cytokine levels in the peri-implant environment were affected by the surface modifications on Ti implants and by treatments with Wnt canonical activators and inhibitors. Peri-implant levels of IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL12, IL-23, CCL2, and TNFalpha were reduced in rough-hydrophilic implants when compared to smooth implants at 3 and 7 days post-implantation (Figs.  9  and  10).  IL-10  and  TGFbeta 1  levels  were  higher  in  roughhydrophilic  implants  when  compared  to  smooth  implants  at  3  days post-implantation (Fig. 9)  and were lower in rough-hydrophilic than smooth  implants  at  day  7  (Fig.  10).  Activation  of  Wnt  canonical signaling by AZD 2858 or SB 216763 increased peri-implant levels of all pro-inflammatory  cytokines  measured  in  both  implants  smooth  and rough-hydrophilic and both day 3 and day 7 (Figs. 9 and 10). In contrast, treatment  with  PNU  74654  decreased  levels  of  TGFbeta 1  on  roughhydrophilic implants at day 3 when compared to vehicle control animals, and IL-6, CCL2, TNFalpha , and TGFbeta 1 on smooth Ti implants at day 7 when compared to vehicle control.

Systemically, circulating levels of IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-12, IL-23, CCL2, and TNFalpha were  higher  on  mice  receiving  smooth  Ti  implants  when compared to mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants at day 3 postimplantation (Fig. 11A). In the other hand, circulating levels of IL-10 were  higher  in  mice  with  rough-hydrophilic  Ti  implants.  Treatment with  activators  of  canonical  Wnt  signaling  (AZD  2858,  SB  216763) increased circulating levels of IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-12, IL-23, CCL2, TNFalpha , and TGFbeta 1, and reduced levels of IL-10 on both Ti implants tested. Inhibition of canonical Wnt signaling reduced circulating levels of IL-10 and TGFbeta 1 in mice with smooth implants, and increased circulating levels of IL-6, IL-12, IL-23, CCL2, and TNFalpha , and decreased IL-10 and TGFbeta 1in mice with rough-hydrophilic Ti implants. 2858 or SB 216763 increased circulating levels of IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-12, IL23, CCL2, TNFalpha ,  and TGFbeta 1 in both Ti implants, an effect that was stronger on rough-hydrophilic Ti than in vehicle-treated mice (Fig. 11B). Treatment with PNU 74654 decreased circulating levels of IL-10 and TGFbeta 1 in mice with smooth Ti implants, and increased IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL12, IL-23, CCL2, and TGFbeta 1.
3.8. Activation of Canonical Wnt signaling increases macrophage sensitivity DAMPs in vitro

To  evaluate  whether  upregulation  of  TLRs  after  canonical  Wnt pathway activation in macrophages increases their sensitivity to DAMPs, macrophages were pre-treated with canonical Wnt activators or an inhibitor and then treated with cell lysates or ECM-cell lysates. Both cell lysates and ECM-cell lysates led to higher levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines  IL-1 beta ,  IL-6,  and  TNFalpha when  compared  to  control  groups (Fig.  12).  Pre-treatment  of  macrophages  with  AZD  2858  led  to  the largest increase in pro-inflammatory cytokines in response to lysates. Similarly, macrophages pre-treated with AZD 2858 or SB 216763 had increased production of pro-inflammatory cytokines when treated with ECM-cell lysates when compared to no treatment and cell lysate treatment. Pre-treatment with PNU 74654 blocked the increase in IL-1 beta , IL-6, and  TNFalpha in  response  to  both  cell  lysate  and  ECM-lysates  when compared to control treatment.
4. Discussion

Macrophage  activation  and  polarization  is  highly  dependent  on biomaterial  surface  characteristics.  We  and  others  have  shown  that macrophages  polarize in  vitro and in  vivo in  response  to  changes  in biomaterial  surface  roughness  and  energy  [2,6,7].  Furthermore,  we have  shown  that  macrophages  increase  Wnt  ligand  production  in response  to  these  surface  properties  and  that  macrophages  failed  to polarize and activate in the absence of Wnt ligands in vitro and in vivo [9].  In  this  work,  we  provide  evidence  that  canonical  Wnt  signaling plays a fundamental role in macrophage polarization by increasing the availability  of  important  interleukin  and  TLRs  that  allows  them  to respond to inflammatory signals and activate them into a pro-inflammatory state creating an inflammatory microenvironment at D. Avery et al.

Figure, 8. Activation of canonical Wnt signaling increases circulating macrophages and T cells in vivo . Immunophenotyping of macrophages and T cells in C57BL/6J mice injected with activators (AZD 2858, SB 216763) or an inhibitor (PNU  74654)  of  canonical  Wnt  signaling  or  vehicle  control.  p < 0.05:  A  vs. respective vehicle, B vs. respective AZD 2858, C vs. respective PNU 74654.
the biomaterial implant site.

Previously, we have shown that the inflammatory microenvironment after biomaterial implantation is modulated by macrophages in response to biomaterial characteristics such as surface roughness and hydrophilicity  [1,2,9,47].  Macrophages  generate  a  pro-inflammatory  environment in response to smooth Ti and will spawn an anti-inflammatory microenvironment  when  rough-hydrophilic  modified  Ti  surfaces  are used  [1,2,9,36,48 -50].  Additionally,  we  have  seen  that  hydrophilic surfaces  will  lead  to  upregulation  of  anti-inflammatory  macrophage differentiation,  while  hydrophobic  surfaces  cause  differentiation  to pro-inflammatory phenotypes [2,6]. We have also identified that macrophages increase expression and production of Wnt ligands initially after biomaterial implantation and in response to biomaterial surface characteristics  [9].  Here,  we  identified  that  expression  of Wnt3a ,  a broadly  study  canonical  Wnt  ligand,  and beta -catenin  ( Ctnnb1 ),  the intracellular  signal  transducer  for  canonical  Wnt  signaling,  are  upregulated in response to smooth and rough Ti surfaces in a time dependent manner with the highest expression at 12  h.  We  attribute  this  early expression as part of the macrophage response to the smooth Ti substrates and suggest that Wnt3a or beta -catenin as important molecules in pro-inflammatory  macrophage  response.  Interestingly, Lrp5 ,  a  membrane  co-receptor  for  canonical  Wnt  ligands,  and Tlr4 ,  a  membrane DAMP and PAMP receptor, are upregulated on smooth and rough surfaces but only following Wnt3a and Ctnnb1 upregulation, suggesting that expression of Wnt ligands and TLRs are dependent on canonical Wnt signaling.  We  have  demonstrated  that  inhibiting  Wnts,  either  with pharmacological inhibition or with a Wls knockout mouse, abgrogates macrophage sensitivity to both physical and biological stimuli, impairing recruitment of immune cells and MSCs to the implantation site [9]. This highlights the importance of Wnt signaling in macrophage polarization and creation of the inflammatory microenvironment.

In this study, we tested the hypothesis that Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling skews macrophage activation and polarization towards a proinflammatory  state.  We  used  smooth  and  rough-hydrophilic  Ti  substrates  as  our  model  since  these  two  surface  modifications  generate opposite  macrophage  phenotypes  [2,6,9].  We  have  observed  that smooth Ti surfaces increase pro-inflammatory macrophage activation, with higher production of pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-1 beta and TNFalpha [2,9].  In  contrast, rough-hydrophilic Ti modifications increase anti-inflammatory  macrophage  activation,  with  higher  production  of anti-inflammatory cytokines like IL-4 and IL-10 [1,2,6,9,51]. Our results showed  that  activation  of  the  Wnt/ beta -catenin  signaling  pathway  in macrophages significantly increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1 beta , IL-6, TNFalpha ), while inhibition of Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling did not affect cytokine production, suggesting that Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling activation enhances  pro-inflammatory  macrophage  polarization  and  microenvironment.  Furthermore,  we  showed  that  reducing  Wnt  canonical signaling activation by silencing Lrp5 resulted in reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine release in response to Ti substrates, particularly on smooth and rough surfaces that produce a stronger pro-inflammatory phenotype. We used RAW 264.7 cell line since these cells have a similar phenotype to macrophages, are a commonly used macrophage-like cell line for biological and biomaterial studies, and are easy to transduce and select over long periods of time [52 -54].

The role of Wnt signaling on macrophages in inflammatory diseases or homeostasis is unclear [55 -61]. Recent reports have associated the activation of canonical Wnt signaling in macrophages to anti-inflammatory mechanisms [62]. Canonical Wnt signaling components modulate inflammatory and immune responses via the interaction with  nuclear  factor  kappa-light-chain-enhancer  of  activated  B  cells (NFkappa B),  and  NFkappa B  also  modulates  the  activity  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling [63]. Studies have shown that canonical Wnt signaling negatively regulates NFkappa B activity in liver, breast, and colon cancer cells [64]. However, these effects were not observed in other cancer cells such head and neck cancer cells [65]. Moreover, Wnt signaling in macrophages is tissue specific. During the response to myocardial infarct, mice lacking Wntless in macrophages, preventing Wnt secretion, exhibited a more anti-inflammatory phenotype; however, in a murine liver injury model, these same mice had the same pro-inflammatory cytokine profile when compared to control [66 -68]. These conflicting results may be due to  variation in Wnt signaling by tissue resident cells and the inflammatory milieu on those tissues and diseases.

The effects of canonical Wnt signaling activation by Wnt3a or inhibitors of GSK3 on macrophages cultured on smooth, rough, or roughhydrophilic were similar to our results on TCPS. An increase of proinflammatory cytokines was observed after treatments activating Wnt/ beta -catenin  signaling  on  all  Ti  surfaces,  with  the  highest  increase  on smooth  Ti  surfaces.  The  reduction  in  pro-inflammatory  cytokines commonly observed on macrophages cultured on rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces was not observed when macrophages were treated with Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling activation agents. These results suggest that D. Avery et al.

smooth  Ti,  confirming  the  idea  that  pro-inflammatory  macrophage activation  and  production  of  pro-inflammatory  microenvironment  is partially mediated by activation of Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling in response to smooth Ti surfaces. Inhibition of Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling increased production  of  anti-inflammatory  interleukins  (IL-4,  IL10)  on  rough surfaces, but did not change on smooth Ti surfaces. These results suggest that  on  rough  and  rough-hydrophilic  Ti  surfaces,  activation  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling results in an increase of pro-inflammatory cytokine production but also in a reduction of anti-inflammatory cytokines, while in  smooth  surfaces  the  reduction  of  the  anti-inflammatory  cytokine production  is  absent.  This  can  be  the  result  of  different  integrin expression or changes in the cytoskeleton in response to microstructured surfaces that combined with the Wnt signaling result in differentially gene expression as seen in other models [69 -75].

Our in vivo studies showed that systemic pharmacological administration of canonical Wnt signaling activators had a pronounced effect on immune cells in circulating blood, increasing levels of macrophages and T cells in comparison to vehicle-treated mice in a homeostatic condition. Activating canonical Wnt signaling also increased CD4 + T cells in bone marrow cells but did not have any effect on CD8 T cells. Similarly, in our model of intramedullary femoral implant, we found that total macrophages, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells were significantly increased, and anti-inflammatory macrophages were reduced in mice treated  with  canonical  Wnt  signaling  activators  when  compared  to vehicle-treated  control  mice  at  3  or  7  days  after  Ti  implantation. Treatment with activators of canonical Wnt signaling resulted in similar outcomes  independent  of  surface  characteristics  of  the  Ti  implants, confirming our in vitro results. Furthermore, inhibition of Wnt/ beta -catenin D. Avery et al.

signaling by PNU 74654 slightly increased total and pro-inflammatory macrophages  and  decreased  anti-inflammatory  macrophages,  and  a strong reduction in T cells was also independently of surface modification. Together, these results highlight the complexity and importance or Wnt signaling on immune cell activation and the inflammatory microenvironment.  Our  results  suggest  that  activation  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling  skews  the  inflammatory  microenvironment  into  a  proinflammatory  state,  but  inhibition  of  the  same  signaling  does  not result in abrogation of the inflammatory response and in fact results also in a reduction of anti-inflammatory macrophages. This can be the result of the effect of Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling on T cells. The early presence of T cells in the implantation site and the changes in polarization state that we have reported previously suggest that T cells are active participants of the inflammatory state and its resolution. In our case, it is possible that  the  drastic  reduction  of  T  cells  by  inhibition  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling  resulted  in  less  immunomodulatory  effect  from  T  cells affecting macrophage polarization towards an anti-inflammatory phenotype.  Several  studies  have  shown  that  T  cell  polarization  and cytokine  production  modulates  macrophage  function.  In  a  model  of rheumatoid  arthritis  has  been  observed  that  direct  contact  between macrophages and T cells results in production of TNFalpha , and removal of T cells from rheumatoid arthritis synovium significant reduced macrophage TNFalpha production [76]. Similarly, IL-10 production in rheumatoid arthritis synovial-membrane mononuclear cells and M -CSF -primed macrophages is activated by interaction with cytokine-stimulated T cells [76]. Wnt signaling plays a fundamental role in the development of T cells  and  proliferation,  cell  adhesion,  and  anti-apoptotic  genes  have been identified as Wnt targets [77]. Canonical Wnt signaling occurs in cells in the thymus, and is most active in the immature double negative stages  [78].  Furthermore,  expression  of  CD4  by  double  positive  thymocytes  is  regulated  in  part  by beta -catenin-TCF  signaling,  and  TCF1 deficient mice express lower levels of CD4 in double positive thymocytes [79,80].

Our results also showed that expression of Il1r1, Tlr1, Tlr2, Tlr4, Tlr5, and Tlr9 were upregulated in response to smooth Ti surfaces, while Il1rn , Il4ra ,  and Il10ra were  upregulated  on  rough-hydrophilic  surfaces. Treatment  with  activators  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin  signaling  upregulated expression of the same receptors that in response to smooth Ti surfaces in a dose dependent fashion. These results suggest that expression of important cytokine and DAMP receptors is partially mediated by canonical  Wnt  signaling  and  that  the  same  signaling  is  involved  in macrophage response to smooth Ti surfaces. Additionally, Il6ra, which recognizes another known pro-inflammatory cytokine, was not affected by  activation  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin  signaling  but  was  upregulated  in response to smooth Ti surfaces, suggesting a Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling independent mechanism. Inhibition of Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling resulted in increased interleukin receptor 4 and 10 expression and reduced Il1r1 and  all  TLRs.  We  also  found  increase  expression  of Lrp5 and Lrp6 , important  co-receptors  in  Wnt/ beta -catenin  signaling,  in  macrophages cultured on smooth Ti surfaces and in response to activation of Wnt/ beta -catenin  signaling.  This  pattern  is  like Axin2 expression,  which  is known to be a target gene during Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling. Lastly, since we observed an upregulation of TLRs in response to Wnt/ beta -catenin activators, we tested the hypothesis that Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling primes macrophages to DAMPs by increasing the availability of TLRs. Our results  showed  a  significant  increase  in  production  of  potent  proinflammatory cytokines (IL-1 beta , IL-6, and TNFalpha ) in response to cell lysates, with the strongest effect on cell-ECM lysates. It has been reported that  TLR  activation  mediates  expression  of Wnt5a ;  however,  less  is known about the regulation of TLRs by Wnt signaling activation [59].
5. Conclusion

Our  results demonstrate an important role  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling in polarizing macrophages towards a pro-inflammatory activation by increasing interleukin receptors and TLRs. Pharmacological activation of Wnt canonical signaling increased local and systemic proinflammatory cytokines, and pharmacological inhibition of Wnt signaling reduced the local and systemic pro-inflammatory environment that smooth Ti implants generated. Our results indicate that Wnt canonical signaling increases TLRs and pro-inflammatory interleukin receptors that skew macrophages towards a pro-inflammatory response, which  may  affect  biomaterial  fate.  Modulation  of  Wnt/ beta -catenin signaling may  be another approach to reduce the initial proinflammatory microenvironment and improve biomaterial-tissue integration.

Full length article
Contribution of alphabeta T cells to macrophage polarization and MSC recruitment and proliferation on titanium implants
Derek Avery a , Lais Morandini a , Melissa Gabriec a , Luke Sheakley a , Matthieu Peralta a , Henry J. Donahue a , Rebecca K. Martin b , Rene Olivares-Navarrete a ,
- a Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Engineering, Virginia Commonwealth University, Richmond, VA, United States
- b Department of Microbiology and Immunology, School of Medicine, Virginia Commonwealth University, Richmond, VA, United States
a r t i c l e i n f o
Article history: Received 11 April 2023 Revised 20 July 2023 Accepted 26 July 2023 Available online 1 August
Keywords: alphabeta T cells CD4 CD8 Macrophages Titanium Surface modifications
1. Introduction

Biomaterials are used in various medical applications, including transplants, drug delivery, and as implants. The immune system plays an essential role in the early inflammatory phase and the long-term success of implants [1-3] . This long-term success is specifically dictated by the degree of wound healing and tissue integration at the implant site. Rapid healing and inflammation resolution are essential for promoting biomaterial-tissue integration [4-7] . Post-implantation, the immune system initiates an inflammatory response, resulting in the recruitment of innate immune cells, such as neutrophils and macrophages, as well as adaptive immune cells, including B cells and T cells [ 5 , 8-17 ].

Corresponding author at: VCU College of Engineering, Department of Biomedical Engineering, 70 S. Madison Street, Room 3328, Richmond, VA 23220. E-mail address: ronavarrete@vcu.edu (R. Olivares-Navarrete) .
a b s t r a c t

Physiochemical cues like topography and wettability can impact the inflammatory response and tissue integration after biomaterial implantation. T cells are essential for immunomodulation of innate immune cells and play an important role in the host response to biomaterial implantation. This study aimed to understand how CD4 + and CD8 + T cell subsets, members of the alphabeta T cell family, polarize in response to smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic titanium (Ti) implants and whether their presence modulates immune cell crosstalk and mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) recruitment following biomaterial implantation. Post-implantation in mice, we found that CD4 + and CD8 + T cell subsets polarized differentially in response to modified Ti surfaces. Additionally, mice lacking alphabeta T cells had significantly more proinflammatory macrophages, fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages, and reduced MSC recruitment in response to modified Ti post-implantation than alphabeta T cell -competent mice. Our results demonstrate that T cell activation plays a significant role during the inflammatory response to implanted biomaterials, contributing to macrophage polarization and MSC recruitment and proliferation, and the absence of alphabeta T cells compromises new bone formation at the implantation site.
Statement of Significance

T cells are essential for immunomodulation and play an important role in the host response to biomaterial implantation. Our results demonstrate that T cells actively participate during the inflammatory response to implanted biomaterials, controlling macrophage phenotype and recruitment of MSCs to the implantation site.
