T cells are lymphoid-derived immune cells studied in pathogenic infection, cancer, autoimmune disorders, and soft tissue injury and repair [18-27] . Differentiation and activation of T cells occur through the expression of CD3 and the T cell receptor (TCR) [28] . The two primary TCRs that distinguish T cell subsets are the alphabeta TCR and gammadelta TCR, which are formed as a result of rearranging alpha -, beta -, gamma -, and delta -chains in the thymus [28-30] . Activation of these TCRs results in differentiation into alphabeta T cells and gammadelta T cells. alphabeta T cells are the most studied T cell subdivisions consisting of two specific subsets: helper T cells (Th) expressing the CD4 receptor and cytotoxic T cells (Tc) expressing the CD8 receptor. alphabeta T cells are involved in the early inflammatory response and have essential roles in bone regeneration and IL-2 production [31-35] . gammadelta T cells remain relatively understudied in comparison to their alphabeta counterparts and do not require antigenpresenting cells to respond to signals from other immune cells [ 36 , 37 ].
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Acta Biomaterialia
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/actbio

A significant obstacle in T cell studies is understanding the activation and roles of various T cell subsets during wound healing. T cells are poorly characterized as to their role in the inflammatory response to biomaterials and wound resolution, and studying the functions of these subsets poses challenges. Helper T cells and cytotoxic T cells can be stimulated to differentiate into various T cell subsets. Helper T cells differentiate into Th1, Th2, Th9, Th17, Th22, and regulatory T cells (Treg), while cytotoxic T cells differentiate into Tc1, Tc2, Tc9, Tc17, Tc22, and Treg [ 28 , 38-40 ]. Differentiation to these subsets depends on cytokines in the inflammatory microenvironment that induce activation of distinct transcription factors, promoting specific phenotypic expression [ 28 , 38-40 ]. Helper T and cytotoxic T cell subsets are commonly identified by the presence of these transcription factors, in addition to specific cytokine production and extracellular cytokine receptor expression [ 28 , 39 , 40 ]. Classically, T cell studies have focused on their roles in cancer, infections, and autoimmune diseases. The respective roles of a few of these T cell subsets are well characterized in response to these conditions [41-45] . Th1 cells have been characterized by the expression of the transcription factor, T-bet, and the production of IFNgamma that promotes a pro-inflammatory response [ 46 , 47 ]. Th2 cells are identified by GATA3 expression and IL-4 production, essential for wound healing and anti-inflammatory regulation [ 45 , 48 ]. CD4 + Tregs are characterized by FOXP3 expression, regulating the inflammatory response through the secretion of IL-10 and TGFbeta [49-51] . Th9 cells are identified by expression of PU.1 and production of IL-9, previously demonstrating a role in generating fibrotic response [ 52 , 53 ]. Th17 cells are characterized by the expression of ROR gamma T and IL-17 production, and this subset is known for enhancing the pro-inflammatory microenvironment in pathologies like osteoarthritis [ 48 , 54 ]. Th22 cells have been identified by AHR expression and secretion of IL-22, demonstrating both fibrotic and regenerative capabilities, dependent upon the tissue where polarization occurs [ 55 , 56 ]. CD8 + T cell subsets are less studied than CD4 + T cells but have been characterized by similar cytokine secretion and transcription factor expression as their CD4 + T cell subset counterparts previously described [ 28 , 39 , 57 ]. The respective roles of alphabeta T cell subsets in response to implantation remain unknown.

We have previously demonstrated that CD4 + T cell subsets at the peri-implant site can be influenced by macrophage response to surface roughness and wettability of Ti implants [5] . T cell polarization depends upon the innate immune cells on the implant surface. Macrophages are innate immune cells that we have previously explored in the biomaterial context [ 5 , 12 , 58-60 ]. Nave macrophages can be polarized into a pro-inflammatory phenotype, identified by the cell surface markers CD80 + /CD206 -, or to an anti-inflammatory phenotype, identified by the markers CD206 + /CD80 -. We have previously identified these populations and their inflammatory function by secretion of specific cytokines and factors [ 5 , 11 , 12 , 59 ]. We have shown that the ablation of macrophages in vivo impaired the recruitment of T cells and MSCs, reducing bone apposition to the implanted biomaterial [12] . In addition, we have shown that the Th2 and Treg subsets are upregulated in response to macrophages on rough-hydrophilic Ti implants, coinciding with higher anti-inflammatory macrophages and exhibiting an anti-inflammatory effect [5] . Th1 and Th17 subsets are reduced in response to macrophages on rough and rough-hydrophilic implants, along with lower pro-inflammatory macrophages, demonstrating a pro-inflammatory phenotype of these subsets [5] . Still, the specific roles of other T cell subsets in the peri-implant microenvironment in response to biomaterial surface modifications are highly understudied.

This study aimed to understand the response of T cell subsets to physical and chemical cues on Ti implants and how they contribute to tissue integration. In addition, understanding how the presence of T cells impacts the immune response and immune cell crosstalk with macrophages and MSCs may elucidate the contribution of alphabeta T cell subsets in the healing response and tissue integration after biomaterial implantation.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Ti disks and implants

Smooth, rough, and rough hydrophilic Ti disks and implants were provided by Institut Straumann AG (Basel, Switzerland), as previously described [ 5 , 12 , 13 , 60 ]. Briefly, 15 mm disks were generated from 1 mm thick unalloyed Ti grade 2. Disks were degreased in an ultrasound acetone bath, then immersed in 2% ammonium fluoride, 2% hydrofluoric acid, and 10% nitric acid to produce smooth Ti surfaces. Rough Ti disks were generated by sand-blasting smooth Ti surfaces with 0.25-0.50 mm coarse-grit corundum, then acid etched in a mixture of HCl and H 2 SO 4 . Smooth and rough Ti disks were rinsed in deionized water and dried after processing. Rough hydrophilic Ti disks were generated with the same protocol as rough Ti disks but rinsed under a nitrogen atmosphere and sealed in an isotonic NaCl solution to prevent air exposure. 4 mm Ti implants were created from 1 mm diameter rods and modified using the same protocols as disks. All Ti disks and implants were sterilized by gamma -irradiation before use.
2.2. Ti disk and implant characterization

Surface roughness was assessed qualitatively using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and quantitatively using confocal microscopy (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um. The arithmetic mean height of the scale limited surface (Sa) was calculated using a moving average Gaussian filter with a cut-off wavelength of 30 um. Mean surface roughness was measured at six different areas of three samples for each surface type. Surface hydrophilicity was assessed by sessile drop contact angle using a Ram-Hart goniometer (Model 100-25a, Ram-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 ul drops of deionized water were measured at six locations per sample (n = 3). Oxide layer composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a PhI50 0 0 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.). Spectra were acquired at a base pressure of 1 x 10-7 Pa using a focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um. The instrument was run in the FAT analyzer mode. The pass energy used for survey scans was 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV for detail spectra. Data were analyzed using the program CasaXPS. The signals were integrated following Shirley background subtraction. Measurements were performed in three separate areas on each sample.
2.3. T cell response to femoral implants in vivo

Twelve-week-old male C57BL/6J mice (Stock #0 0 0 6 64, The Jackson Laboratory, Bar Harbor, ME) were anesthetized using isoflurane delivered in O 2 gas and monitored for unconsciousness by pedal reflex, as described previously [ 12 , 60 , 61 ]. The skin overlaying the knee was opened, and the patellar tendon was bisected longitudinally utilizing a scalpel and retracted to reveal the femoral condyles. A dental bur was used to access the medullary canal. A cylindrical smooth, rough, or rough hydrophilic Ti implant ( = 1mm) was then press fit (n = 6 mice per implant condition), and the implant placement was confirmed by x-ray. Mice were administered 1mg/kg buprenorphine ER-LAB (ZooPharm, Laramie, WY) before anesthesia recovery to relieve postoperative pain for 72 h. Animals were monitored until initial ambulation and every 24 h afterward. All animals had access to food and water ad libitum for the duration of the study. No signs of infection were seen in this study. Animal procedures were used per a protocol approved by the Virginia Commonwealth University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (AD10 0 01108).

Mice were euthanized by CO 2 asphyxiation on postoperative days 3, 7, or 14. Femur bones and the inguinal lymph node nearest the side of implantation (draining lymph node) were harvested. To isolate peri-implant tissue, femurs were cut mid-shaft, and the implant and surrounding marrow were flushed using Accutase (Innovative Cell Technologies, San Diego, CA). Isolated bone marrow was incubated in ACK Lysing Buffer (Quality Biological Inc., Gaithersburg, MD) to remove erythrocytes. Lymph nodes were homogenized and flushed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) without Ca ++ or Mg ++ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA). Cells isolated from bone marrow and lymph nodes were washed with staining buffer (BioLegend, San Diego, CA) before staining for flow cytometry analysis.
2.4. Flow cytometry

Single-cell suspensions from lymph nodes and bone marrow were used for flow cytometry analysis. Before fluorescent staining, red blood cells were lysed with ACK lysis buffer (ThermoFisher) and stained with Zombie NIR TM Fixable Viability Kit (BioLegend). Next, Fc receptors were blocked with TruStain FcX (anti-CD16/32, BioLegend). Cell suspensions were then incubated with fluorescent antibodies (BioLegend) to identify populations of alphabeta T cells (CD3 + /TCR alphabeta + ), CD4 + T cells (CD3 + /CD4 + ), CD8 + T cells (CD3 + /CD8 + ), neutrophils (CD45 + /CD11b + /Ly6G + /Ly6C + ), macrophages (CD45 + /CD11b + /MHCII + /F4/80 + , Mac + ), proinflammatory macrophages (Mac + /CD80 + /CD206 -), antiinflammatory macrophages (Mac + /CD206 + /CD80 -), dendritic cells (CD45 + /CD11b + /MHCII + /CD11c + /F4/80 -), and MSCs (CD45 -/Sca1 + /CD90 + /CD105 + ). Additionally, helper and cytotoxic T cell subsets were identified utilizing intracellular transcription factors, including Th1 (CD3 + /CD4 + /Tbet + ), Th2 (CD3 + /CD4 + /Gata3 + ), Th9 (CD3 + /CD4 + /PU.1 + ), Th17 (CD3 + /CD4 + /ROR gamma T + ), Th22 (CD3 + /CD4 + /AhR + ), CD4 + Treg (CD3 + /CD4 + /FoxP3 + ), Tc1 (CD3 + /CD8 + /Tbet + ), Tc2 (CD3 + /CD8 + /Gata3 + ), Tc9 (CD3 + /CD8 + /PU.1 + ), Tc17 (CD3 + /CD8 + /ROR gamma T + ), and CD8 + Treg (CD3 + /CD8 + /FoxP3 + ). Fluorescence Minus One (FMO) controls were used to establish the gating for all the populations analyzed (Supplementary Figures 1 and 2 ). Samples were analyzed using a BD LSRFortessa-X20 Flow Cytometer (BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA) instrument, and a minimum of 250,0 0 0 events were collected per sample. Results were analyzed using FlowJo software (FlowJo LLC, Ashland, OR).
2.5. T Cell receptoralpha (Tcra) knockout mouse

To understand the role of alphabeta T cells in response to modified Ti, transgenic mice null for TCRalpha ( Tcra -/ -) (The Jackson Laboratory, Stock # 002116) were crossed with C57BL/6J ( Tcra + / + , The Jackson Laboratory, Stock #0 0 0 6 64) for ten generations to obtain same genetic background. Twelve-week-old male Tcra + / + or Tcra -/ -mice were used for experiments.
2.6. Macrophage isolation

Primary murine macrophages were differentiated from bone marrow harvested from 12-week-old male Tcra + / + or Tcra -/ -mice, as described previously [ 11 , 12 , 60 , 61 ]. Mice were euthanized by CO 2 asphyxiation, and femur bones were removed. The bone marrow was flushed with PBS and treated with ACK buffer (ThermoFisher Scientific). Nave macrophages were derived by culturing cells in RPMI 1640 (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin50 mcg/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), and 30 ng/mL macrophage colony-stimulating factor (M-CSF, BioLegend) for 7 days at 37 deg C, 5% CO2, and 100% humidity. On day 7, macrophages were detached with Accutase (Innovative Cell) for use in subsequent experiments.
2.7. T Cell isolation and activation

Primary murine T cells were differentiated from splenocytes harvested from 12-week-old male Tcra + / + or Tcra -/ -mice. Mice were euthanized by CO 2 asphyxiation, and the spleens were removed. The spleens were homogenized, flushed with PBS, and treated with ACK buffer. Nave T cells were derived by seeding splenocytes at a density of 1,0 0 0,0 0 0 cells/well in 6-well plates and cultured in RPMI 1640 Medium (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 mcg/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), 0.05 mM beta -mercaptoethanol (ThermoFisher Scientific), 25 uL of prewashed and resuspended Dynabeads Mouse T-Activator CD3/CD28 (ThermoFisher Scientific), and 30 U/mL recombinant mouse IL-2 (BioLegend) for three days at 37 deg C, 5% CO2, and 100% humidity. On day 3, activated T cells were harvested for use in subsequent experiments.
2.8. Effect of macrophages on T cell subset polarization in vitro

To understand the effect of macrophage response to modified Ti on T cell polarization, nave macrophages were seeded in 24-well plates on either tissue culture plastic substrate (TCPS), smooth, rough, or rough hydrophilic Ti disks at a density of 20 0,0 0 0 cells/well. Macrophages were added to the cell culture prior to T cells to mimic recruitment and activation within the inflammatory microenvironment in vivo. After 24 h, activated T cells were added to the macrophage cultures at a cell seeding density of 30 0,0 0 0 cells/well. We chose this ratio of macrophages to T cells to replicate the in vivo microenvironment at 3 days postimplantation. All cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 Medium (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 mcg/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), 0.05 mM beta -mercaptoethanol (ThermoFisher Scientific). Macrophages and T cells were allowed to interact for 48 h before cells were collected for flow cytometry analysis. Untouched splenocytes cultured with macrophages on TCPS were used for controls. Single-cell suspensions from cell cultures were incubated with TruStain FcX (anti-CD16/32, BioLegend). Cell suspensions were then incubated with fluorescent antibodies to identify T helper (Th1, Th2, Th9, Th17, Th22, Treg) or cytotoxic (Tc1, Tc2, Tc9, Tc17, Treg) subsets as previously described. A minimum of 10 0,0 0 0 events were collected per sample. Results were analyzed using FlowJo software.
2.9. Impact of T-cell polarization on macrophage phenotype in vitro

To understand the impact of polarized T cells on the inflammatory phenotype of macrophages, we performed coculture experiments with Tcra + / + or Tcra -/ -mouse cells. Activated T cells were added to macrophages seeded on TCPS, smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic Ti substrates either indirectly, utilizing a 1 um pore transwell insert, or directly as described prior. Nave macrophages isolated from Tcra + / + or Tcra -/-and seeded on TCPS, smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic Ti substrates without T cells but with transwell inserts were used as control. Macrophages and T cells were allowed to interact for 48 h before media was removed, and macrophages were detached with Accutase (Innovative Cell Technologies) for flow cytometry analysis. Single-cell suspensions from cell cultures were incubated with TruStain FcX (anti-CD16/32, BioLegend). Cell suspensions were then incubated with fluorescent antibodies to identify pro-inflammatory macrophages (Mac + /CD80 + /CD206 -) and antiinflammatory macrophages (Mac + /CD206 + /CD80 -). A minimum of 50,0 0 0 events were collected per sample. Results were analyzed using FlowJo software.
2.10. Macrophage-T cell-MSC co-culture model

To understand the role of macrophage-T cell crosstalk in MSC recruitment and proliferation, we conducted co-culture experiments between macrophages and activated T cells isolated from Tcra + / + or Tcra -/ -mice, as described above. At 36 h of co-culture, conditioned media was removed, and fresh starvation media was added (RPMI 1640, 1% FBS) and incubated for 12 h. After incubation, conditioned media was collected and transferred onto a new 24-well plate.

For MSC recruitment, fluorescently tagged (CellTracker TM Green CMFDA Dye, ThermoFisher Scientific) adipose-derived MSCs (m17.ASC, Sigma-Aldrich) were seeded at 50,0 0 0 cells/cm 2 in the upper chamber of 8 um pore transwell inserts in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 1% FBS, with the lower chamber containing the conditioned media from macrophage-T cell co-cultures. After 12 h, transwells were removed. MSC recruitment in response to factors released by macrophages and T cells cultured on modified Ti surfaces was quantified by cell fluorescence using the Synergy HTX Multimode Reader (Biotek Instruments). Nave macrophages isolated from Tcra + / + or Tcra -/-and seeded on TCPS, smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic Ti substrates without T cells but with transwell inserts were used as control.

For MSC proliferation, adipose-derived MSCs (Sigma-Aldrich) were seeded at 50,0 0 0 cells/cm 2 in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 1% FBS. After 12 h, MSCs were incubated with ClickiT TM EdU (ThermoFisher Scientific) stain for 1 h, then the conditioned media from macrophage-T cell co-cultures on Ti surfaces was added. After 12 h, cells were fixed, permeabilized, and treated with Click-iT TM EdU buffer and reaction cocktail. Cells were analyzed by flow cytometry for the formation of new DNA. A minimum of 20,0 0 0 events were collected per sample.
2.11. Effect of alphabeta T cell ablation in de novo bone formation on titanium implants

To understand the role of alphabeta T cells in de novo bone formation, 12-week-old male Tcra + / + or Tcra -/ -mice (n = 6) were anesthetized using isoflurane/O 2 gas and instrumented to access the medullary canal and smooth, rough, or rough-hydrophilic Ti rods were implanted as described before. After 14 days, mice were euthanized by CO 2 asphyxiation and fixed in 10% formalin (ThermoFisher) for subsequent microCT analysis.
2.12. MicroCT analysis

To understand the role of alphabeta T cells in the formation of new bone post-implantation, the distal femoral metaphysis was scanned at a resolution of 4032 x 2688 pixels (voxel size of 7.00um) over 180 deg using a high-resolution 0.5mm aluminum filter, scanning energies of 60kV and 200 uA, 630 ms exposure time. An image was acquired every 0.15 deg using SkyScan 1276 (Bruker, Billerica, MA). Serial two-dimensional cross-sections were assembled into 3D reconstructions and analyzed using SkyScan software and CTAn (Bruker).

De-novo bone formation was quantified in a tubular region of interest (ROI) in the distal portion of each femur. The tubular ROI encompassed a length of 3.75mm, an outer diameter of 1.12mm, and an inner diameter of 1.00mm, creating a volumetric sleeve aligned with the centroid of the implant. All images were individually evaluated, and the ROI mask was applied to each image. A threshold of 10 0 0.0 0-250 0.0 0 Hounsfield units in the ROI was used to identify bone. Morphometric indices of bone volume selected for representation from the binarized volume of interest (VOI) included the bone volume / tissue volume (BV/TV) ratio, which quantifies the amount of bone relative to the size of the VOI. 3D representations of bone formation were modeled using Dragonfly (Object Research Systems, Montral, QC).
2.13. Statistical analysis

Data are presented as mean +/- SD. Statistical analysis was performed using Prism9 (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA). Data were first subjected to Shapiro-Wilk normality test. Results from this test indicate that the data were normally distributed. A onefactor, equal analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to test the null hypothesis that group means were equal at a significance level of alpha = 0.05, with post hoc Tukey's HSD test for multiple comparisons.
3. Results
3.1. Ti Disks and implant characterization

SEM imaging demonstrated increased surface roughness and topography on rough and rough hydrophilic Ti compared to smooth Ti ( Fig. 1A ). Confocal microscopy quantitatively showed higher roughness for rough (Sa = 3.33 um) and rough hydrophilic Ti (Sa = 3.30 um) in comparison to smooth Ti (Sa = 0.61 um). Surface roughness between rough and rough hydrophilic Ti surfaces did not demonstrate quantitative or qualitative differences. Water contact angle measurements ( Fig. 1B ) showed that rough Ti ( theta = 86 deg ) was the most hydrophobic and rough-hydrophilic Ti ( theta = 0 deg ) was the most hydrophilic. Smooth Ti was normal ( theta = 66 deg ) compared to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti. The wettability of our Ti surfaces was correlated with the carbon content of the oxide layer. Surface chemical composition showed that rough hydrophilic Ti had the lowest carbon content in the oxide layer, while rough Ti had the highest carbon content in the oxide layer ( Fig. 1C ).
3.2. Modified Ti surfaces alter innate immune cell populations and MSCs in vivo

We explored the response of innate immune cells to physiochemical cues within the bone marrow. At 3 days postimplantation, total and pro-inflammatory macrophages did not show differences in response to modified Ti implants ( Fig. 2 ). Antiinflammatory macrophages were higher on rough and rough hydrophilic Ti implants, and neutrophils and dendritic cells were lower on rough hydrophilic Ti. At 7 days post-implantation, total macrophages, pro and anti-inflammatory macrophages, neutrophils, and dendritic cells were lower on rough and rough hydrophilic implants. These innate immune cells were the lowest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants at day 14 postimplantation.

MSCs did not show differences in response to modified Ti at 3 days post-implantation. However, at day 7 post-implantation, MSCs were the highest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. On day 14 post-implantation, MSCs remained higher on rough hydrophilic Ti than other modified Ti implants.

Fig. 1. Characterization of smooth, rough, and rough hydrophilic Ti samples. (A) Surface topography analyzed qualitatively via scanning electron microscopy. B) Measurement of water contact angle. (C) Chemical composition of the material oxide layer for each sample.
|    | Smooth   | Rough   | Rough-Hydro   |
3.3. Physiochemical cues affect alphabeta T cell response in vivo

Similarly, we explored the role of modified Ti surfaces in alphabeta T cell response within the bone marrow. At 3 days post-implantation, alphabeta + , CD4 + , and CD8 + T cells were higher on rough hydrophilic Ti implants ( Fig. 3 ). At day 7 post-implantation, CD4 + T cells remained higher on rough hydrophilic Ti. In contrast, alphabeta T cell and CD8 + T cell populations were reduced on rough hydrophilic Ti compared to smooth Ti. Finally, at 14 days post-implantation, alphabeta + , CD4 + , and CD8 + T cell populations were the lowest on rough hydrophilic Ti.
3.4. Helper T cells have differential response to the peri-implant microenvironment in vivo

To investigate the role of helper T cell subsets in the healing response post-biomaterial implantation, we immunophenotyped CD4 + T cell subsets from bone marrow in the peri-implant mi- croenvironment. At postoperative day 3, Th2, Th22, and CD4 + Treg populations were significantly higher in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants, while Th1, Th2, and Th17 subsets were lower ( Fig. 4 ). On day 7, Th1, Th2, Th9, Th17, and CD4 + Treg subsets were significantly lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. Th22 populations were lower in response to rough Ti implants on day 7. On day 14 post-implantation, Th1, Th2, Th9, and Th17 populations were the lowest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. Th22 and CD4 + Treg populations were lower in response to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti on day 14.

Fig. 2. Modified Ti implants alter populations of macrophages, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and MSCs. Flow cytometry analysis of peri-implant bone marrow at 3-, 7-, and 14-days post-implantation. p < 0.05: # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti, A vs. day 3, B vs. day 7.

Fig. 3. alphabeta T cells decrease over time in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti implants. Flow cytometry analysis of alphabeta T cells, CD4 + T cells, and CD8 + T cells from peri-implant bone marrow at 3-, 7-, and 14-days post-implantation. p < 0.05: # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti, A vs. day 3, B vs. day 7.
3.5. Cytotoxic T cells have distinct response to the peri-implant microenvironment in vivo

Similarly, we immunophenotyped cytotoxic T cell subsets from peri-implant bone marrow. At day 3 post-implantation, all CD8 + T cell subsets were the highest in response to smooth Ti implants ( Fig. 5 ). At postoperative day 7, Tc1, Tc2, Tc9, and Tc17 subsets were significantly lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants. At postoperative day 14, similarly to day 7, Tc1, Tc2, and Tc17 subsets were the lowest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. CD8 + Tregs did not show significant differences between modified Ti at day 7
and 14, and Tc9 did not display differences between groups at day 14.
3.6. Surface properties differentially modulate generation of helper T cell subsets in vivo

Next, we analyzed T helper cell polarization within the lymph node to understand the production and activation of T cells in the inflammatory response to modified Ti implantation. The inguinal lymph node, nearest the side of implantation, was immunophenotyped for analysis of helper T cell subsets. At postoperative day 3, Th1, Th9, and Th17 subsets were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants ( Fig. 6 ). Th2 populations were higher in response to rough hydrophilic Ti, while CD4 + Treg populations were higher in response to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti. Th22 did not demonstrate significant differences between modified Ti on day 3. At day 7 post-implantation, similarly to day 3, Th1, Th9, and Th17 subsets were significantly lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. On the contrary, Th2, Th22, and CD4 + Treg subsets were the highest in rough hydrophilic Ti implants on day 7 post-implantation. Th1 cells were reduced lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti on day 14. Interestingly, Th2, Th9, Th22, and CD4 + Treg populations showed similar reductions in response to rough hydrophilic Ti but significantly higher populations on rough Ti on day 14 post-implantation. Th17 was lower in response to both rough and rough hydrophilic Ti. At day 21 post-implantation, Th1 and Th2 subsets were the lowest in response to all modified Ti implants, with no significant difference between groups. Th9, Th17, and CD4 + Treg subsets were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. Like day 14, Th22 was higher in response to rough Ti on day 21 post-implantation.

Fig. 4. Th1, Th9, and Th17 subsets polarize the least in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti implants. Flow cytometry analysis of CD4 + T cell subsets from peri-implant bone marrow at 3-, 7-, and 14-days post-implantation. p < 0.05: # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti, A vs. day 3, B vs. day 7.
3.7. Modified Ti implants modulate cytotoxic T cell subset production in vivo

Like CD4 + T cell subsets, we were interested in the generation of cytotoxic T cell subsets within the inguinal lymph node near the implantation site. At day 3 post-implantation, Tc1 was lower in response to rough Ti implants, while Tc9 and Tc17 subsets were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti ( Fig. 7 ). Tc2 populations were the highest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti at 3 days post-implantation. Tc9 and Tc17 were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. CD8 + Tregs did not show significant changes between groups at postoperative day 3. On day 7, Tc1, Tc9, and Tc17 populations were the highest among all time points but lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants. Tc2 populations were higher in response to rough Ti implants. CD8 + Tregs were the highest in response in rough hydrophilic Ti on day 7 post-implantation. On day 14, Tc1, Tc2, and Tc9 subsets were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants. Tc17 populations were similarly lower in response to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti. CD8 + Tregs were higher in rough Ti implants at postoperative day 14. Finally, at postoperative day 21, Tc1 populations did not show a significant difference between groups but were the lowest compared to previous time points. Tc2, Tc17, and CD8 + Tregs were the lowest in response to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti implants. Contrary, Tc9 populations were higher in response to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti at 21 days post-implantation.

Fig. 5. CD8 + T cell subset polarization is the lowest in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Flow cytometry analysis of CD8 + T cell subsets from peri-implant bone marrow at 3-, 7-, and 14-days post-implantation. p < 0.05: # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti, A vs. day 3, B vs. day 7.
3.8. Helper T cell phenotypic expression is influenced by physiochemical properties in co-culture

To investigate immune cell crosstalk and modulation of helper T cell phenotype by macrophages cultured Ti with physiochem- ical cues, macrophages were seeded on modified Ti surfaces in vitro along with activated CD3 + /CD28 + T cells . Th1, Th9, and Th17 subsets were the highest in response to macrophages cultured on smooth Ti surfaces ( Fig. 8 ). Th2, Th22, and CD4 + Treg subsets were the highest in response to macrophages cultured on rough hydrophilic Ti surfaces. Additionally, Th22 and CD4 + Treg populations were higher in response to macrophages on all modified Ti surfaces than on TCPS.

Fig. 6. Surface modifications on Ti implants affect CD4 + T cell subset polarization in the lymph node. Flow cytometry analysis of CD4 + T cell subsets in the lymph node at 3-, 7-, and 14-days post-implantation. p < 0.05: # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti, A vs. day 3, B vs. day 7.

Fig. 7. Tc2 and CD8 + Tregs polarize more in response to rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants in the early inflammatory phase. Flow cytometry analysis of lymph nodes at 3-, 7-, and 14-days post-implantation. p < 0.05: # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti, A vs. day 3, B vs. day 7.
3.9. Modified Ti induces specific cytotoxic T-cell phenotypes in co-cultures in vitro

Similarly, we explored cytotoxic T-cell polarization in response to macrophages cultured on a modified Ti surface. Tc1, Tc9, and Tc17 subsets were the highest in response to macrophages on smooth Ti and the lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti ( Fig. 9 ). Tc2 and CD8 + Treg populations were the highest in re- sponse to macrophages cultured on rough hydrophilic Ti surfaces. Similarly, CD8 + Tregs were the highest in response to macrophages on rough hydrophilic Ti and demonstrated elevations on all surfaces compared to TCPS.

Fig. 8. Th2, Th22, and CD4 + Tregs polarize the highest in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti in macrophage-T cell co-cultures. Flow cytometry analysis of T cells and macrophages co-cultured on either TCPS, smooth Ti, rough Ti, or rough hydrophilic Ti for 24 h. p < 0.05: # vs. TCPS, $ vs. smooth Ti, % vs. rough Ti.

Fig. 9. Tc2 and CD8 + Tregs polarize more in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Flow cytometry analysis of T cells and macrophages co-cultured on either TCPS, smooth Ti, rough Ti, or rough hydrophilic Ti for 24 h. p < 0.05: # vs. TCPS, $ vs. smooth Ti, % vs. rough Ti.
3.10. Deficiency of alphabeta T cells significantly upregulates innate immune cells and attenuates MSC recruitment in vivo

To understand the significance of alphabeta T cells post-implantation, we immunophenotyped innate immune cells in Tcra -/ -mice. We compared these results against our immunophenotyping data from Tcra + / + mice from 3.1 ( Supplementary Figure 3 ).

At 3 days post-implantation, total macrophages, neutrophils, and MSCs from Tcra -/ -mice did not demonstrate differences from Tcra + / + mice or between modified Ti implants ( Fig. 10 ). Dendritic cells from Tcra -/ -mice were lower in response to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti but did not show differences from Tcra + / + mice.

Pro-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were the lowest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti, but higher on all modified Ti surfaces when compared to Tcra + / + mice. Anti-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were the highest in response to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti implants but significantly lower than anti-inflammatory macrophages in Tcra + / + mice on all modified Ti implants.

At day 7 post-implantation, total macrophages and proinflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti but significantly higher on all modified Ti than Tcra + / + mice ( Fig. 11 ). Anti-inflammatory macrophages Tcra -/ -mice were significantly lower than Tcra + / + mice and did not show differences between modified Ti. Similarly, neutrophils from Tcra -/ -mice did not demonstrate differences between modified Ti implants but were higher than neutrophils from Tcra + / + mice. Dendritic cells from Tcra -/ -mice also did not show differences between groups but were lower in response to smooth and rough Ti than Tcra + / + mice and higher in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. MSCs from Tcra -/ -mice were higher in response to rough hydrophilic Ti but significantly lower than Tcra + / + mice on all surfaces.

Fig. 10. Mice without alphabeta T cells demonstrate higher pro-inflammatory macrophages, lower anti-inflammatory macrophages, and less responsiveness to modified Ti implants. Flow cytometry analysis of macrophages, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and MSCs in mice with or without alphabeta T cells from peri-implant bone marrow at day 3 postimplantation. p < 0.05: A vs. Tcra + / + , # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti.

Fig. 11. Mice lacking alphabeta T cells display higher populations of neutrophils and macrophages, as well as lower recruitment of MSCs in response to modified Ti implants. Flow cytometry analysis of macrophages, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and MSCs in mice with or without alphabeta T cells from peri-implant bone marrow on day 7 post-implantation. p < 0.05: A vs. Tcra + / + , # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti.

At day 14 post-implantation, total macrophages, proinflammatory macrophages, and neutrophils from Tcra -/ -mice were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. Still, they remained higher in Tcra + / + mice on all surfaces than ( Fig. 12 ). Anti-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were higher in response to rough hydrophilic Ti surfaces but significantly lower in response to all modified Ti than Tcra + / + mice. Dendritic cells from Tcra -/ -mice were lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. Additionally, dendritic cells from Tcra -/ -mice were lower in response to smooth and rough Ti implants when compared to Tcra + / + mice but were higher than Tcra + / + mice in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. MSCs from Tcra -/ -mice demonstrated no differences between modified Ti implants and were significantly lower than MSCs from Tcra + / + mice.

Fig. 12. Mice without alphabeta T cells exhibit higher pro-inflammatory macrophages and lower MSC recruitment in response to modified Ti implants. Flow cytometry analysis of macrophages, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and MSCs in mice with and without alphabeta T cells from peri-implant bone marrow on day 14 post-implantation. p < 0.05: A vs. Tcra + / + , # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti.
3.11. Presence of alphabeta T cells enhances macrophage phenotype in vitro

To discern the magnitude of immune cell communication of T cells on macrophage phenotype and proliferation, we performed coculture experiments on modified Ti surfaces, as previously described. We cultured Tcra + / + macrophages alone, directly, or indirectly with Tcra + / + T cells. Similarly, we cultured Tcra -/ -macrophages alone, directly, or indirectly with Tcra -/ -T cells.

Pro-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra + / + mice cultured independently were higher in response to smooth Ti ( Fig. 13 ). Proinflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were the highest in response to smooth Ti compared to TCPS and Tcra + / + . Proinflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice are also higher in response to rough Ti than Tcra + / + . Contrary, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough hydrophilic Ti for both Tcra + / + and Tcra -/ -, with no difference between groups. Antiinflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were higher than Tcra + / + in response to smooth Ti only.

Assessing the direct co-culture of macrophages and T cells, proinflammatory macrophages from Tcra + / + were significantly higher in response to smooth Ti and lower in response to rough hydrophilic Ti ( Fig. 13 ). These differences between modified Ti surfaces were parallel in pro-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice but exhibited significantly lower populations on all surfaces compared to Tcra + / + . Anti-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra + / + mice were the highest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti, like the Tcra + / + macrophages cultured alone. Anti-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were lower on TCPS and all modi- fied Ti surfaces than Tcra + / + . Anti-inflammatory macrophages were the highest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti but still significantly lower than Tcra + / + .

Examining the indirect co-cultures of macrophages and T cells on modified Ti surfaces demonstrated similar results to the direct co-cultures assessed prior. Pro-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra + / + mice were the highest in response to smooth Ti ( Fig. 13 ). Like direct co-cultures, pro-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were lower in response to all modified Ti surfaces when compared to Tcra + / + . Pro-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice were also significantly higher in response to smooth Ti than TCPS. For anti-inflammatory macrophages, antiinflammatory macrophages from Tcra + / + mice were the highest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti. Anti-inflammatory macrophages from Tcra -/ -were also higher on rough hydrophilic Ti but significantly lower on all modified Ti surfaces compared to Tcra + / + .
3.12. alphabeta T cell presence increases MSC recruitment in vitro

To understand whether T cells play a role in the recruitment of MSCs, we performed coculture experiments between MSCs and the conditioned media from macrophages, with or without T cells. We performed these cocultures with cells from Tcra -/ -or Tcra + / + mice. Macrophages from Tcra + / + mice cultured without T cells demonstrated the lowest recruitment of MSCs on smooth Ti and the highest recruitment on rough hydrophilic Ti ( Fig. 14A ). Macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice did not demonstrate any differences from Tcra + / + , with Tcra -/ -macrophages recruiting the least MSCs on smooth Ti and the most MSCs on rough hydrophilic Ti.

Examining macrophages from Tcra + / + cultured with Tcra + / + T cells, MSC recruitment was the highest on rough hydrophilic Ti compared to TCPS ( Fig. 14B ). MSC recruitment was the lowest on smooth Ti for Tcra + / + . Macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice cultured with Tcra -/ -T cells recruited fewer MSCs on TCPS than Tcra + / + . MSC recruitment by Tcra -/-macrophages and T cells was the lowest on smooth Ti and the highest on rough hydrophilic Ti. Macrophages and T cells from Tcra -/ -mice recruited significantly less MSC on all modified Ti surfaces when compared to Tcra + / + .

Cells from either Tcra alpha + / + or Tcra -/ -mice were cultured on either TCPS, smooth Ti, rough Ti, or rough hydrophilic Ti for 24 h. p < 0.05: A vs. Tcra + / + , # vs. TCPS, $ vs. smooth Ti, % vs. rough Ti.
3.13. Absence of alphabeta T cells significantly attenuates proliferation of MSCs

To understand the significance of T cells for MSC proliferation, we cocultured the conditioned media from macrophages with or without T cells with MSCs in a proliferation assay to detect the formation of new DNA. The conditioned media for these experiments was collected from Tcra -/ -or Tcra + / + mouse cells. Macrophages from Tcra + / + mice showed the lowest proliferation of MSCs in response to TCPS and smooth Ti surfaces ( Fig. 15A ). MSC proliferation was the highest in response to conditioned media from cells cultured on rough-hydrophilic Ti. MSC proliferation was not significantly different from the conditioned media from macrophages from Tcra -/ -mice.

The conditioned media from macrophages and T cells cultured on rough hydrophilic Ti from Tcra + / + mice demonstrated the highest proliferation of MSCs ( Fig. 15B ). MSC proliferation was similar between TCPS and smooth Ti from the conditioned media from cells from Tcra + / + mice. MSC proliferation was the lowest in re- sponse to the conditioned media from macrophages and T cells from Tcra -/ -mice cultured on TCPS. MSC proliferation was also lower with the conditioned media from Tcra -/ -macrophages and T cells on all modified Ti surfaces compared to cells from Tcra + / + mice. Additionally, MSC proliferation in response to conditioned media from Tcra -/ -mice did not display differences between modified Ti.

3.14. Absence of alphabeta T cells reduces bone implant contact in response to modified Ti implants

To understand if T cells impact new bone formation around Ti implants, we analyzed bone-implant contact 14 days postimplantation. Tcra -/ -mice produced significantly lower bone around all modified Ti implants than Tcra + / + mice ( Fig. 16 ). Tcra -/ -mice showed lower peri-implant bone on hydrophobic Ti implants (smooth or rough) than Tcra + / + mice. The highest bone contact was observed in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants in Tcra + / + mice.
4. Discussion

Biomaterial physiochemical cues play a pivotal role in the recruitment and polarization of immune cells. We and others have recently characterized the innate immune response to different implanted biomaterials. However, the role of T cells during the inflammatory phase and biomaterial integration is unclear. This study demonstrates that helper and cytotoxic T-cell recruitment, activation, and proliferation are contingent upon Ti surface roughness and wettability. Our data indicate that T cells are sensitive to changes in the peri-implant microenvironment and actively participate in the inflammatory milieu, either enhancing or immunomodulating the inflammatory microenvironment by contributing to macrophage phenotype. Furthermore, our data indicate that conventional T cells are required to recruit MSCs since ablation of T cell receptor alpha significantly reduces MSC recruitment to the peri-implant tissue.
T cells are classically recognized for their active role in the immunological response to infections and autoimmune disorders.
Bone Implant Contact

Fig. 16. Bone implant contact is increased in alphabeta -T cell competent mice. Analysis of bone formation around modified Ti implants in Tcra + / + or Tcra -/ -mice after 21-days post-implantation. p < 0.05: A vs. Tcra + / + , # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti.

However, their role in tissue healing and regeneration is less clear. Recently, T cells have been shown to play an essential role in soft tissue healing and repair, including fibrosis [ 15 , 18 , 34 , 35 , 51 , 62 , 63 ]. While secondary lymphoid organs, such as lymph nodes, are the primary sites of initiation of T cells, the tissue where the T cells reside alters the antigen receptor repertoire compared with those in the systemic immune system, resulting in tissue-specific T cell responses [ 18 , 19 , 27 , 51 , 64 , 65 ]. Studies have shown the paradoxical effects of T cells during tissue healing and regeneration. T cell depletion increased mortality in a model of liver regeneration, but a lack of T cells led to scarless skin regeneration [ 66 , 67 ]. In biomaterial implantation, T cells are usually associated with chronic inflammatory responses and fibrotic tissue formation in a process known as foreign body response [ 16 , 17 , 68 ]. However, recent studies have shown that T cells, specifically some CD4 + subsets, are positively associated with the pro-regenerative or healing response [63] . Our results emphasize the significance of alphabeta T cells in the inflammatory response to biomaterials showing that alphabeta T cells are essential to modulate innate immune cell response and resolve the inflammatory response. Specifically, in Tcra -/ -mice, we observed a significant increase of pro-inflammatory macrophages and a reduction of anti-inflammatory macrophages in all time points measured compared to alphabeta -competent mice. Conventional T cells, specifically Th2 and Tregs, are essential for suppressing inflammatory innate immune cells and promoting anti-inflammatory macrophage polarization by producing anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-4, IL-10, and IL-13 [ 5 , 51 , 69 ]. In vitro and in vivo studies have shown the effect of Th2 and Treg in producing a more anti-inflammatory microenvironment and skewing macrophage activation towards an anti-inflammatory phenotype [ 5 , 38 ]. In vivo studies have also shown the immunomodulatory effect of Treg adoptive transfer in graft and solid organ transplantation [70-73] . Tregs suppress CD4 + and CD8 + T cells directly and indirectly and antigen-presenting cell proliferation and function [74] . Tregs possess different contactdependent mechanisms, such as the expression of perforin and granzymes, which directly disrupt the target cell membrane resulting in apoptosis and expressing inhibitory molecules (CTLA-4, PD-1) which suppress dendritic cells, macrophages, and other T cells [ 75 , 76 ]. Tregs also secrete immunomodulatory cytokines such as IL-10, IL-35, and TGFbeta 1 that suppress alphabeta T cells and polarize macrophages towards an anti-inflammatory phenotype [77-79] . Altogether, we show that a lack of alphabeta T cells skews macrophages into a pro-inflammatory polarization and inhibits suppression of the inflammatory response.

CD4 + T cells play a central role in the function of the immune system by interacting with B cells to produce humoral immunity, regulate macrophage function, and orchestrate immune responses against a wide array of pathogens regulating/suppressing immune responses to control autoimmune disorders. These functions are achieved through different CD4 + subsets originating from nave CD4 + T cells. These subsets differentiate by a pattern of signals, including TCR and cytokines, they receive during the initial interaction with antigen-presenting cells [80] . While dendritic cells are considered the most potent antigen-presenting cells and are recognized for T cell activation, most studies focusing on biomaterial physicochemical properties on dendritic cells have been performed in vitro , and the in vivo focus has been on generating immune tolerance or specific immunological response such as the use of biomaterials as vaccines. However, the role of dendritic cells in the inflammatory response and biomaterial integration remains unknown [81-83] . Examining CD4 + T cells in peri-implant bone marrow, we show that CD4 + T cell subsets polarize differently in response to differences in Ti surface properties. Our data showed that smooth and rough Ti implants increase CD4 + T cell polarizations towards Th1, Th9, and Th17 subsets, while rough-hydrophilic Ti implants preferentially polarize CD4 + T cells into Th2, Th22, and Treg subsets. Furthermore, we identified changes in polarization of these T cell subsets post-implantation in the lymph node, demonstrating that T cells are affected external to the peri-implant microenvironment. Heightened activation at 7 days post-implantation may result from cytokines released into circulation that induce T cell polarization or dendritic cells trafficked to the lymph node after the initial inflammatory response to stimulate T cell activation [ 5 , 84 ]. In this study, we identified Treg populations based on the transcription factor FoxP3. However, other publications have reported Treg populations CD25 as an additional marker to FoxP3 or as substitute for FoxP3 [85-88] . The use of CD25 as a marker in T cells was introduced when Sakaguchi demonstrated that peripheral tolerance was mediated by a small subset of T cells (CD4 + CD25 + ), which upon adoptive transfers were able to suppress the development of autoimmunity in mice [89] . While CD25 is broadly used to identify Tregs, activation of T cells results in expression of CD25 independent of the T cell polarization [ 90 , 91 ]. Furthermore, mice genetically deficient in Foxp3 fail to generate a CD4 + CD25 + Tregs, and in when FoxP3 is ectopically expressed in mice CD4 + CD25 -Tregs show suppressive activity [ 49 , 92 , 93 ].

Interestingly, smooth and rough Ti implants also induce a more robust pro-inflammatory macrophage activation, and roughhydrophilic Ti implants cause more anti-inflammatory macrophage phenotypes in vitro and in vivo [5] . Our results showed that at early time points, dendritic cell recruitment is similar between Tcra + / + and Tcra -/ -mice, with higher recruitment in response to smooth than rough or rough-hydrophilic Ti implants. However, we observed decreased dendritic cell presence in response to smooth and rough and increased dendritic cells in rough-hydrophilic implants in Tcra -/ -mice compared to alphabeta -competent mice at later time points. These results suggest that other inflammatory stimuli initially regulate dendritic cell recruitment.

alphabeta T cells regulate their presence at the implantation site by physical contact or secretion of important inflammatory cytokines. Th1 cells are essential in secreting IFNgamma and TNFalpha , inducing other immune cell activation, and promoting macrophage nitric oxide production [ 94 , 95 ]. Here, we show that Th1 polarization corresponds with higher pro-inflammatory macrophages in response to smooth Ti. Furthermore, we also identified Th17 cells in the periimplant tissue of smooth Ti implants. Th1 and Th17 have previously shown a similar response to clinically used orthopedic implants, polarizing significantly more in response to polyetheretherketone (PEEK) and stainless steel than Ti implants [61] . This Th1/Th17 effect has been observed previously where exacerbated levels of IL-6, IL-8, IL-17, IFNgamma , and TNFalpha have been found in the fibrotic tissue surrounding silicone implants [62] . Th17 cells secrete pro-inflammatory IL-17 that enhances the pro-inflammatory microenvironment by signaling neutrophils and macrophages to upregulate inflammatory factors. Results from our study confirm that Th17 cells recruit and support more neutrophils since Th17 cells were elevated in smooth and rough Ti implants, which were also the implants with elevated neutrophil presence over time. Moreover, Th17 can also induce bone destruction by stimulating osteoclastogenesis and secretion of TNFalpha and IL-6 [ 96 , 97 ].

Th2 response has been shown to induce a pro-regenerative environment on tissue-derived scaffolds [63] . Th2 is important for reducing inflammation via IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13 secretion and activating anti-inflammatory macrophages to promote wound healing [ 38 , 45 , 95 ]. We have also demonstrated that Th2 polarization correlates with higher anti-inflammatory macrophages. The Treg subset can reduce Th17-mediated IL-17 production by producing IL10, eliciting an immunomodulatory effect by suppressing inflammatory T cells and innate immune cells [ 62 , 96 , 98 ]. Tregs have also previously been shown to inhibit osteoclasts and restore bone mass [98] . The balance between Th17 and Treg activation has previously been explored due to the opposing effects of these subsets on bone resorption [96] . Our study shows higher polarization of Th17 in response to smooth Ti in the late inflammatory time course, suggesting that the pro-inflammatory microenvironment induced by smooth Ti may cause a Th17/Treg imbalance. Th9 and Th22 are subsets we measured in our study, but these subsets have not yet been shown in the context of the inflammatory response to implantation or bone healing. The subsets and their secreted cytokines produce a contradictory inflammatory phenotype, and their effects appear tissue-specific [ 52 , 53 , 55 , 56 ]. In our study, Th9 shows a pro-inflammatory phenotype. In contrast, Th22 shows an antiinflammatory phenotype, demonstrated by polarization in response to modified Ti implants in vivo and in in vitro co-culture experiments.

Like CD4 + T cells, CD8 + T cells were the highest in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti at 3 days post-implantation, decreasing at later time points in response to rough hydrophilic Ti, suggesting a clear active role early in the inflammatory response [ 39 , 57 ]. While these changes can result from the inflammatory microen- vironment in the peri-implant tissue, these results also suggest that bone remodeling is reduced in rough-hydrophilic implants. A study using tibial fracture healing as a model for endogenous regeneration strongly associated the enhanced presence of CD8 + T cells with delayed healing [99] . Furthermore, it was observed that CD8 + T cells could be triggered independently of costimulatory molecule interaction (antigen-presenting cells). Also, it can be activated even without T cell receptor/antigen engagement by pro-inflammatory cytokines released by innate cells in response to damage-associated molecular patterns [100-102] . Animal studies have also shown that elevated levels of CD8 + T in hematomas are associated with poor fracture healing [103] . Rapid reduction of peri-implant CD8 + T cells after initial inflammatory response may promote osseointegration and bone formation. CD8 + T cell subsets are understudied within the context of biomaterial implantation but have previously demonstrated osteoprotective properties, such as inhibition of osteoclastogenesis [104-106] . Analysis of osteoarthritic synovial fluid has shown higher polarization of pro-inflammatory subsets Tc1 and Tc17 and lower presence of anti-inflammatory subset Tc2 [57] . Chronic elevations of CD8 + T cells have also been associated with a lack of bone formation by increased secretion of TNFalpha and IFNgamma . IFNgamma induced enhanced and altered extracellular matrix formation resulting in fibrosis [103] . Additionally, it has been shown that TNFalpha and IFNgamma block MSC-mediated bone formation in a mouse model and human MSCs [107] .

The significance of immune cell-cell communication between macrophages and T cells has been demonstrated by our in vitro studies. The macrophage response to modified Ti surfaces affects the inflammatory phenotype of these various alphabeta T cell subsets since T cells do not directly interact with the biomaterial substrate. Our in vitro co-culture system mimics the peri-implant microenvironment at day 3 post-implantation, in which T cells are recruited to the implantation site and interact with macrophages adhered to the biomaterial [ 5 , 16 , 17 , 108 , 109 ]. Macrophage-T cell crosstalk has been analyzed previously in asthma pathogenesis, demonstrating that macrophages release factors that promote T cell migration and enhance inflammatory response [110] . In our study, higher Th1, Th9, Th17, Tc1, Tc9, and Tc17 polarization coincides with a higher presence of pro-inflammatory macrophages on smooth Ti. Similarly, anti-inflammatory macrophages on rough hydrophilic Ti are higher along with higher polarization of Th2, Th22, CD4 + Tregs, Tc2, and CD8 + Treg subsets. Studies of CD8 + subsets are commonly performed by coating TCPS with co-stimulants such as CD3 and CD28 antibodies, yielding lower pro-inflammatory polarization than seen in this study. We aimed to mimic the physiological response, with T cells interacting with macrophages adhered to modified Ti substrates. We and others have shown that macrophages are sensitive to physicochemical changes on the biomaterial surface, including substrate stiffness, which skewed macrophages into a pro-inflammatory phenotype with increased secretion of inflammatory cytokines that affect T cell polarization [ 5 , 58 , 59 , 111-113 ].

Macrophages can induce T cell polarization through the release of specific cytokines, including IL-12 (Th1 polarization), IL-4 (Th2 polarization), and IL-6 (Th17 polarization) [114] . Conversely, prior literature has shown that macrophages can be regulated by polarized T cells and their secreted soluble factors [ 16 , 17 , 109 , 110 ]. Th1 cells can produce MIP-1 alpha , MIP-1 beta , and ATAC, promoting activation of macrophages, while Th2-derived IL-4 can promote the production of free radicals via the formation of foreign body giant cells by fusing macrophages [115-117] . A previous study examining interactions between the Tregs and macrophages found that Tregderived IL-10 and TGFbeta activated anti-inflammatory macrophages and suppressed the pro-inflammatory response by macrophages [118] . Moreover, IFNgamma secretion by T cells increases nitric oxide production and proliferative activity of macrophages [ 119 , 120 ].

Our in vitro studies show that T cells amplify pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory macrophage populations. The presence of alphabeta T cells positively contributed to macrophage proliferation, significantly enhancing both pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory macrophage populations when cultured with competent alphabeta T cells in comparison to cells from Tcra -/ -mice. Together, T cells and macrophages demonstrate a feedback loop dynamic in activating and polarizing each other by secretion of various soluble factors in the inflammatory reaction to modified Ti surfaces.

MSC recruitment and proliferation are essential for bone healing and osseointegration after implantation. Our in vivo studies demonstrated that mice deficient in alphabeta T cells produce less bone around all modified Ti implants, with no significant differences between smooth and rough modifications. Mice lacking alphabeta T cells produce a more pro-inflammatory microenvironment and have compromised recruitment and/or proliferation of MSCs in the periimplant bone marrow. Previous studies have investigated the functional role of MSCs in helper T cell suppression, but cell-cell communication between alphabeta T cells and MSCs is not fully understood. CD8 + T cells have been reported to secrete specific chemokines involved in MSC recruitment in vitro and in vivo [ 64 , 116 , 117 , 121 , 122 ]. Another study involving autologous transplantation of Tregs with MSCs found that Tregs promoted both proliferation and survival of MSCs in an in vivo model of chronic ischemia compared to MSCs alone [123] . Our in vitro experiments demonstrate similar results, showing that the presence of alphabeta T cells significantly increases MSC recruitment and proliferation in response to modified Ti surfaces. This suggests that alphabeta T cells may release factors that enhance MSC recruitment and proliferation more than macrophages alone. While we have shown previously that macrophages are required for MSC recruitment to the injury site, here we show that alphabeta T cells may promote synergy with macrophages to amplify the recruitment and proliferation of MSCs [12] . Taken together, T cells may have an essential role in generating chemokines specific to MSC recruitment and proliferation at the injury site that result in new bone formation around implanted materials [ 121 , 124 , 125 ].
5. Conclusion

Our results demonstrate that alphabeta T cell subsets play an important role in the inflammatory response to Ti implantation through modulation of innate immune cells and recruitment and proliferation of MSCs that facilitate tissue integration and bone formation. Additionally, alphabeta T cells enhance the responsiveness of innate immune cells and MSCs to modified Ti. The polarization of these alphabeta T cell subsets may impact the healing and inflammatory resolution rate. Studying the individual functions of these T cell subsets may provide an immunomodulatory approach to promote a proregenerative microenvironment in outcomes involving biomaterial implantation.

CD4 + and CD8 + T cells reduce inflammation and promote bone healing in response to titanium implants
Derek Avery, Lais Morandini, Luke Sheakley, Melissa Grabiec, Rene Olivares-Navarrete
Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Engineering, Virginia Commonwealth University, 70 S. Madison Street, Room 3328, Richmond, VA 23220, United States
a r t i c l e i n f o
Article history: Received 27 November 2023 Revised 11 March 2024 Accepted 24 March 2024
Available online 29 March 2024
Keywords: T cells CD4 CD8 Titanium
Surface modifications
1. Introduction

Titanium (Ti) and its alloys are the material of choice for bonedwelling implants due to their mechanical properties and high biocompatibility [1-4] . Osseointegration, the generation of functional new bone around the implant, is a complex process that requires a timely cascade of events orchestrated by immune cells [46] . Damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) resulting from the surgical procedure initiate the chemotaxis of innate immune cells such as neutrophils and macrophages. We have demonstrated that neutrophils and macrophages are highly responsive to changes in biomaterial physicochemical properties such as surface roughness, chemistry, and hydrophilicity [ 1 , 7-14 ]. The initial interac-
Corresponding author. E-mail address: ronavarrete@vcu.edu (R. Olivares-Navarrete) .
a b s t r a c t

T cells are adaptive immune cells essential in pathogenic response, cancer, and autoimmune disorders. During the integration of biomaterials with host tissue, T cells modify the local inflammatory environment by releasing cytokines that promote inflammatory resolution following implantation. T cells are vital for the modulation of innate immune cells, recruitment and proliferation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), and formation of functional tissue around the biomaterial implant. We have demonstrated that deficiency of alphabeta T cells promotes macrophage polarization towards a pro-inflammatory phenotype and attenuates MSC recruitment and proliferation in vitro and in vivo. The goal of this study was to understand how CD4 + and CD8 + T cells, subsets of the alphabeta T cell family, impact the inflammatory response to titanium (Ti) biomaterials. Deficiency of either CD4 + or CD8 + T cells increased the proportion of proinflammatory macrophages, lowered anti-inflammatory macrophages, and diminished MSC recruitment in vitro and in vivo . In addition, new bone formation at the implantation site was significantly reduced in T cell-deficient mice compared to T cell-competent mice. Deficiency of CD4 + T cells exacerbated these effects com pared to CD8 + T cell deficiency. Our results show the importance of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells in modulating the inflammatory response and promoting new bone formation in response to modified Ti implants.
Statement of significance

CD4 + and CD8 + T cells are essential in modulating the peri-implant microenvironment during the inflammatory response to biomaterial implantation. This study shows that deficiency of either CD4 + or CD8 + T cell subsets altered macrophage polarization and reduced MSC recruitment and proliferation at the implantation site.

tion between innate immune cells and biomaterials results in a modification of the inflammatory microenvironment rich in cytokines and chemokines that recruit other immune cells, including T cells [ 8 , 11 , 15 ]. Studies from our group have shown that ablation of macrophages either by clodronate liposomes or by administration of AP20187 in the Macrophage Fas-Induced Apoptosis (MaFIA) transgenic mouse model severely reduces the recruitment of T cells and MSCs to the site of injury [11] . While the role of innate cells in biomaterial integration is clear, the role of adaptive immune cells is less clear.

T cells are classically activated by antigen-presenting cells (APCs), such as dendritic cells and macrophages, and activation occurs through the T cell receptor (TCR) and CD3 coreceptor. T cells can also be activated via innate immune cell production of proinflammatory cytokines, autonomous of antigen presentation [1517] . After activation, T cells polarize into various subsets that re-
