1. Introduction
Full length article

Tregs and Th17 cells, CD4 + T cell subsets, have demonstrated a role in MSC recruitment, proliferation, and differentiation. In a prior co-culture model between CD4 + T cells and MSCs, the presence of lymphocytes led to higher alkaline phosphatase production by MSCs, indicating differentiation towards osteogenic lineage [ 43 , 81 ]. Tregs have been shown to accelerate the healing of bone via increased MSC osteogenic differentiation and mineral deposition in a critical-sized cranial defect model [81] . TGFbeta 1, a signature Treg cytokine, has demonstrated a function to reduce bone resorption and promote new bone formation, as TGFbeta 1 stimulates the proliferation and differentiation of MSCs to osteoblasts and reduces apoptosis of these cells [ 82 , 83 ]. Several in vivo studies performed in dogs and minipigs have demonstrated that recombinant TGFbeta 1 treatment on implants composed of Ti and Ti alloys significantly increases osseointegration and new bone formation [ 82 , 84 ]. In our study, levels of TGFbeta 1 within macrophage-T cell conditioned media were significantly lower in mice deficient in CD4 + T cells. We also observed reduced MSC recruitment and proliferation in Cd4 -/ -mice. Our results suggest that CD4 + T cells may contribute to the recruitment and proliferation of osteoprogenitor cells through TGFbeta 1. Furthermore, in a model of chronic ischemia, transplantation of MSCs with Tregs into the myocardium showed that Tregs promote the viability and proliferation of MSC post-transplantation [85] .

Th17 cells have traditionally demonstrated a pro-inflammatory phenotype, with previous findings showing that IL-17A stimulates osteoclastogenesis, leading to bone destruction [ 27 , 28 , 43 , 86-89 ]. However, a murine model utilizing IL-17A-deficient mice found that lack of this cytokine resulted in delayed bone healing [69] . IL-17A secretion has been shown to contribute to MSC differentiation to osteoblasts and CXCL12 production, a powerful chemoattractant for recruiting immune cells and MSCs to the injury site [ 90 , 91 ]. An in vitro model for rheumatoid arthritis found that coculture of fibroblast-like synoviocytes with CD4 + T cells or treat- ment with recombinant IL-17A significantly enhanced the production of CXCL12 [91] . CD4 + T cells alone are not a primary source of CXCL12. However, CD4 + T cells may synergize with other cell types, like macrophages, to upregulate CXCL12 secretion and promote MSC recruitment and proliferation. In our study, we demonstrated significantly attenuated secretion of IL-17A and CXCL12 from mice deficient in CD4 + T cells, suggesting that CD4 + T cells play a fundamental role either in the secretion of these molecules or influencing other cells, such as macrophages, in producing these molecules. CXCL12 is a chemoattractant for other immune cells and has been associated with the progression of the inflammatory response. It has also been recognized as a critical regulator of cell migration during tissue healing and regeneration.

Additionally, previous studies have shown that CD8 + T cells inhibit MSC bone formation; therefore, mice deficient in CD4 + T cells may have an exacerbated reduction of MSC recruitment and new bone formation due to both the lack of CD4 + T cells and heightened levels of CD8 + T cells [ 16 , 92 , 93 ]. We note this compensation of either CD4 + or CD8 + T cells for the deficiency of the other subset in our transgenic knockout models as a limitation of this study (Supplemental Fig. 3).

Our study has an application to other clinical states outside of implantation. Absence or diminishment of CD4 + T cells is the diagnosis of patients with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). Studies have found that patients with HIV have significantly delayed or nonunion healing of bone fractures [94] . These patients also have higher levels of serum TNFalpha , which we see in Cd4 -/ -mice in our study [94] . Prior literature has speculated that an upregulation of TNFalpha can promote desensitization to the inflammatory response, inhibiting the healing process [ 71 , 95 ]. Alterations in helper and cytotoxic T cells are characteristics of many conditions that can impact healing and regeneration in response to injury. In the context of bone healing and implantation, the changes in these T cell populations should be considered in patients who fall under this category to promote the long-term success of implants or fracture healing.
5. Conclusion

Our results show the importance of both CD4 + and CD8 + T cells in the inflammatory response to Ti implantation. Deficiency of either of these alphabeta T cell subsets results in a skewed innate immune cell profile in the inflammatory microenvironment and reduced new bone formation. These results demonstrate that CD4 + T cells play a more significant immunomodulatory role in neutrophil clearance, macrophage polarization, recruitment and proliferation of MSCs, and promoting bone-implant contact at the peri-implant site. CD8 + T cells demonstrate similar results to CD4 + T cells but do not appear to impact innate immune cells and MSCs to the same degree. Both alphabeta T cell subsets play an essential role in the sensitivity of innate immune cells to surface topography and wettability on Ti implants. Future approaches involving modulation of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells or their effector subsets may be an effective therapeutic strategy to promote accelerated healing and improve osseointegration post-implantation.

Titanium (Ti) and its alloys are the material of choice for bonedwelling implants due to their mechanical properties and high biocompatibility [1-4] . Osseointegration, the generation of functional new bone around the implant, is a complex process that requires a timely cascade of events orchestrated by immune cells [46] . Damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) resulting from the surgical procedure initiate the chemotaxis of innate immune cells such as neutrophils and macrophages. We have demonstrated that neutrophils and macrophages are highly responsive to changes in biomaterial physicochemical properties such as surface roughness, chemistry, and hydrophilicity [ 1 , 7-14 ]. T cells are adaptive immune cells essential in pathogenic response, cancer, and autoimmune disorders. During the integration of biomaterials with host tissue, T cells modify the local inflammatory environment by releasing cytokines that promote inflammatory resolution following implantation. T cells are vital for the modulation of innate immune cells, recruitment and proliferation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), and formation of functional tissue around the biomaterial implant. We have demonstrated that deficiency of alphabeta T cells promotes macrophage polarization towards a pro-inflammatory phenotype and attenuates MSC recruitment and proliferation in vitro and in vivo. The goal of this study was to understand how CD4 + and CD8 + T cells, subsets of the alphabeta T cell family, impact the inflammatory response to titanium (Ti) biomaterials. Deficiency of either CD4 + or CD8 + T cells increased the proportion of proinflammatory macrophages, lowered anti-inflammatory macrophages, and diminished MSC recruitment in vitro and in vivo . In addition, new bone formation at the implantation site was significantly reduced in T cell-deficient mice compared to T cell-competent mice. Deficiency of CD4 + T cells exacerbated these effects com pared to CD8 + T cell deficiency. Our results show the importance of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells in modulating the inflammatory response and promoting new bone formation in response to modified Ti implants.

journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/actbio lease cytokines into the inflammatory microenvironment, altering the inflammatory milieu [18] . The two major subsets of T cells are helper T cells (CD4 + ) and cytotoxic T cells (CD8 + ), differentiated through the expression of the CD4 or CD8 coreceptor. CD4 + and CD8 + T cells, subsets of alphabeta T cells, are commonly studied in the context of infection, cancer, autoimmune disorders, skin healing, and bone disorders [18-22] . Additionally, T cells play a role in wound healing and fibrotic tissue formation and appear to have tissue-specific functions [23-26]

CD4 + T cells are the most significant immune cells in orchestrating adaptive immune responses. In pathogenic infection and cancer, CD4 + T cells are essential for antibody production by B cells for antigen recognition on foreign bodies for neutralization or elimination by the immune system. Nave CD4 + T cells can be polarized into Th1, Th2, Th9, Th17, Th22, and CD4 + regulatory T cell (Treg) subsets. CD4 + T cells have previously demonstrated significance in tissue injury, with subsets such as Th1 and Th17 promoting a pro-inflammatory microenvironment through secretion of IFNgamma , IL-17, and TNFalpha , inducing pro-inflammatory macrophage polarization and osteoclastogenesis [27-29] . On the other hand, CD4 + T cell subsets Th2 and Treg reduce and modulate inflammation through the production of IL-4, IL-10, and TGFbeta . Th2 cells also promote anti-inflammatory macrophage polarization, and Tregs suppress inflammatory cells in the foreign body response. CD4 + T cells also promote activation of CD8 + T cells via secretion of cytokines IL-2 and IFNgamma [ 20 , 30 ]. Like CD4 + T cells, CD8 + T cells can be differentiated into Tc1, Tc2, Tc9, Tc17, and CD8 + Treg subsets. These cells produce the same inflammatory cytokines as their CD4 + T cell counterpart. CD8 + T cells are considerably more abundant than CD4 + T cells, but their role in the inflammatory response to biomaterial implantation is unknown. T cells have been explored briefly in fracture healing and bone regeneration, as elevations in proportions of CD8 + T cells relative to CD4 + T cells have been associated with delayed or poor healing in these applications [16] . Despite these findings, the significance of CD4 + T cells and CD8 + T cells in the inflammatory response to biomaterial implantation remains understudied.

We have previously demonstrated the role of alphabeta T cells in the inflammatory response to biomaterial implantation. CD4 + and CD8 + T cell polarization is impacted by Ti physicochemical cues such as roughness and wettability in vivo [ 8 , 15 ]. Additionally, we have demonstrated that deficiency of alphabeta T cells results in higher pro-inflammatory macrophage phenotype, lower antiinflammatory macrophages, and significantly reduced recruitment and proliferation of MSCs in response to modified Ti [15] . Furthermore, we have demonstrated that the deficiency of alphabeta T cells significantly reduces new bone formation post-implantation [15] . Despite these findings, the individual role of the alphabeta T cell subsets, CD4 + and CD8 + T cells, remains understudied in response to biomaterial implantation.

The aim of this study was to understand how macrophage polarization, MSC recruitment and proliferation, and new bone formation are impacted by CD4 + or CD8 + T cell deficiency in response to modified Ti implants. Elucidating the role of these individual subsets in the foreign body response will demonstrate the significance of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells in the healing response and bone regeneration post-implantation.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Ti disks and implants

Smooth, rough, and rough hydrophilic Ti disks and implants were provided by Institut Straumann AG (Basel, Switzerland), as previously described [ 14 , 15 ]. In brief, 15 mm disks were created from 1 mm thick unalloyed titanium grade 2. Disks were degreased in an ultrasound acetone bath and then immersed in a 2% ammonium fluoride/ 2% hydrofluoric acid/ 10% nitric acid bath to elicit smooth Ti surfaces. Smooth Ti disks were sand-blasted with 0.250.50 mm coarse-grit corundum, then acid etched in a solution of HCl and H2 SO4 to generate rough Ti disks. After processing, smooth and rough Ti disks were washed in deionized water and dried. To create rough hydrophilic Ti disks, the same surface modification was applied as was used for rough Ti disks, but following modification, disks were rinsed under a nitrogen atmosphere and sealed in an isotonic NaCl solution to prevent air exposure. 1 mm diameter Ti rods were used to generate 4 mm implants and modified using the same protocols as disks. gamma -irradiation was used to sterilize all Ti disks and implants before use.
2.2. Titanium disk and implant characterization

Scanning electron microscopy was used to qualitatively assess surface roughness (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and quantitatively using confocal microscopy (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um, as described previously [ 13 , 15 ]. A moving average Gaussian filter with a cut-off wavelength of 30 um was used to calculate the arithmetic mean height of the scalelimited surface (Sa). Six areas of three samples for each surface type were measured to calculate mean surface roughness. Sessile drop contact angle was utilized to assess surface hydrophilicity using a Ram-Hart goniometer (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). Measurements using 5 ul drops of deionized water were performed at six locations per sample (n = 3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a PhI50 0 0 VersaProbe spectrometer was used to determine the composition of the oxide layer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc., Chanhassen, MN). Spectra were acquired at a base pressure of 1 x 10 -7 Pa using a focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 u m. The instrument was run in the FAT analyzer mode. The pass energy used for survey scans was 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV for detail spectra. Data were analyzed using the program CasaXPS. The signals were integrated following Shirley background subtraction. Measurements were performed in three separate areas on each sample.

2.3. CD4 and CD8 knockout mouse

To demonstrate the significance of CD4 + T cells and CD8 + T cells in the response to modified Ti, transgenic mice null for the CD4 coreceptor ( Cd4 -/ -, The Jackson Laboratory, Stock # 002663) were crossed with C57BL/6J (WT, The Jackson Laboratory, Stock #0 0 0 6 64) mice for ten generations to obtain same genetic background. Similarly, transgenic mice null for the CD8 coreceptor ( Cd8 -/ -, The Jackson Laboratory, Stock # 002665) were crossed with WT mice for ten generations to obtain the same genetic background.
2.4. CD4 + and CD8 + T cell response to femoral implants in vivo

Twelve-week-old male Cd4 -/ -, Cd8 -/ -, and WT mice were anesthetized using isoflurane delivered in O2 gas and monitored for unconsciousness by pedal reflex as described previously [ 14 , 15 ]. The femoral condyles were revealed by opening the skin overlaying the knee and using a scalpel to bisect the patellar tendon longitudinally. A surgical dental bur was used to access the medullary canal, and a cylindrical smooth, rough, or rough hydrophilic Ti implant ( = 1mm) was then press fit (n = 6 mice per implant condition).

The implant placement was confirmed by x-ray. To relieve postoperative pain, mice were administered 1mg/kg buprenorphine ERLAB (ZooPharm, Laramie, WY) before anesthesia recovery for 72 h. Animals were monitored until initial ambulation and every 24 h afterward. All animals had access to food and water ad libitum for the duration of the study. No signs of infection were seen in this study. Animal procedures were performed in accordance with a protocol approved by the Virginia Commonwealth University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (Protocol: AD10 0 01108).

On postoperative days 3, 7, or 14, mice were euthanized by CO2 asphyxiation, and femur bones and blood were harvested. Periimplant tissue was isolated by cutting the femur mid-shaft, and the implant and surrounding marrow were flushed using Accutase (Innovative Cell Technologies, San Diego, CA). Isolated bone marrow was incubated in ACK Lysing Buffer (Quality Biological Inc., Gaithersburg, MD) to remove erythrocytes from the marrow isolates. Cells isolated from bone marrow were washed with staining buffer (BioLegend, San Diego, CA) before staining for flow cytometry analysis.
2.5. Flow cytometry

Single-cell suspensions from bone marrow were used for flow cytometry analysis. Before fluorescent staining, red blood cells were lysed with ACK lysis buffer (ThermoFisher), and Fc receptors were blocked with TruStain FcX (antiCD16/32, BioLegend). Cell suspensions were then incubated with fluorescent antibodies (Supplemental Table 1) to identify populations of neutrophils (CD45 + /CD11b + /Ly6G + /Ly6C + ), macrophages (CD45 + /CD11b + /MHCII + /CD11c -/F4/80 + ), proinflammatory macrophages (CD45 + /CD11b + /MHCII + /F4/80 + / CD80 + /CD206 -), anti-inflammatory macrophages (CD45 + /CD11b + / MHCII + /F4/80 + /CD206 + /CD80 -), CD4 + T cells (CD45 + /CD11b -/ CD3 + /CD4 + ), CD8 + T cells (CD45 + /CD11b -/CD3 + /CD8 + ), and MSCs (CD45 -/Sca-1 + /CD90 + /CD105 + ) (BioLegend). Viability dye (BioLegend), single color, and fluorescence minus one control were used to establish the gating strategy, as we recently showed [15] . Samples were analyzed using a BD LSRFortessa-X20 Flow Cytometer (BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA), and a minimum of 250,0 0 0 events were collected per sample. Results were analyzed using FlowJo software (FlowJo LLC, Ashland, OR).
2.6. Impact of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells on inflammatory cytokine production

To understand the role of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells in cytokine production in response to modified Ti implantation, analysis of the cytokine microenvironment in the peri-implant tissue was performed by flushing the implant and surrounding marrow with 500 u L chilled PBS without Ca ++ or Mg ++ . Samples were vortexed for 2 min and centrifuged at 500g for 10 min. Supernatants were transferred into 1.5 mL tubes and stored at -80 deg C. Supernatants from peri-implant tissues were thawed, and levels of IL-2, IL-4, IL5, IL-6, IL-9, IL-10, IL-13, IL-17A, IL-17F, IL-22, IFNgamma , and TNFalpha were measured using Mouse T Helper Cytokine Panel LEGENDplex (BioLegend) following manufacturer's instructions.

2.7. Macrophage isolation

Primary murine macrophages were differentiated from bone marrow harvested from 12-week-old male WT mice as described previously [ 14 , 15 ]. Mice were euthanized by CO2 asphyxiation, and femur bones were removed. PBS and treated with ACK buffer (ThermoFisher Scientific) were used to flush bone marrow. Cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 u g/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), and 30 ng/mL macrophage colony stimulating factor (M-CSF, BioLegend) for four days at 37 deg C, 5% CO2, and 100% humidity. On day 4, macrophages in culture were supplemented with fresh media. Accutase (Innovative Cell) was used to detach macrophages on day 7 for use in subsequent experiments.
2.8. T cell isolation and activation

Splenocytes harvested from 12-week-old Cd4 -/ -, Cd8 -/ -, and WT male mice were used for primary murine T cell differentiation as described previously [15] . Mice were euthanized by CO2 asphyxiation, and spleens were removed. The spleens were homogenized, flushed with PBS, and treated with ACK buffer. Nave T cells were derived by seeding splenocytes at a density of 1,0 0 0,0 0 0 cells/well in 6-well plates and cultured in RPMI 1640 Medium (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin50 u g/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), 0.05 mM beta -mercaptoethanol (ThermoFisher Scientific), 25 uL of prewashed and resuspended Dynabeads Mouse T-Activator CD3/CD28 (ThermoFisher Scientific) and 30 U/mL recombinant mouse IL-2 (BioLegend) at 37 deg C, 5% CO2, and 100% humidity. Activated T cells were harvested after three days for use in subsequent experiments.
2.9. Impact of CD4 + and CD8 + T cell polarization on macrophage phenotype in vitro

To understand the role of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells on the inflammatory phenotype of macrophages. We performed coculture experiments with cells from Cd4 -/ -, Cd8 -/ -, and WT mice. Nave macrophages were seeded in 24-well plates on either TCPS, smooth, rough, or rough hydrophilic Ti disks at a density of 20 0,0 0 0 cells/well. After 24 h, T cells from Cd4 -/ -or Cd8 -/ -mice were added to the macrophage cultures at a cell seeding density of 30 0,0 0 0 cells/well. All cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 Medium (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 u g/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), and 0.05 mM beta -mercaptoethanol (ThermoFisher Scientific). After 48 h of cell interaction, media was removed, and macrophages were detached with Accutase (Innovative Cell Technologies) for flow cytometry analysis. Single-cell suspensions were incubated with TruStain FcX (anti-CD16/32, BioLegend). Cell suspensions were then incubated with fluorescent antibodies to identify pro-inflammatory and antiinflammatory macrophages as described above. A minimum of 50,0 0 0 events were collected per sample. Results were analyzed using FlowJo software.
2.10. Macrophage-T cell-MSC co-culture model

To understand CD4 + and CD8 + T cell deficiency on the crosstalk between macrophage and T cells for MSC recruitment and proliferation, we conducted co-culture experiments between macrophages and activated T cells isolated from Cd4 -/ -, Cd8 -/ -, or WT mice as described above. After 36 h of co-culture, conditioned media was removed, and starvation media was added (RPMI 1640, 1% FBS). At 12 h of incubation, conditioned media was collected and transferred onto a new 24-well plate. Adipose-derived MSCs (m17.ASC, Sigma-Aldrich) were fluorescently tagged (CellTracker TM Green CMFDA Dye, ThermoFisher Scientific) and seeded at 50,0 0 0 cells/cm 2 on an 8 um pore transwell inserts in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 1% FBS. Transwells were removed after 12 h, and MSC migration was quantified using the Synergy HTX Multimode Reader (Biotek Instruments) to measure cell fluorescence. Cell fluorescence was quantified at an excitation wavelength of 485 nm and an emission wavelength of 520 nm.

For MSC proliferation, adipose-derived MSCs (Sigma-Aldrich) were seeded at 50,0 0 0 cells/cm 2 in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 1% FBS. After 12 h, MSCs were incubated with Click-iT TM EdU (ThermoFisher Scientific). Conditioned media from macrophage-T cell co-cultures on Ti surfaces was added after 1 h. After 12 h, cells were fixed, permeabilized, and treated with ClickiT TM EdU buffer and reaction cocktail. Cells were analyzed by flow cytometry for the formation of new DNA. A minimum of 20,0 0 0 events were collected per sample.

We also analyzed secreted proteins involved in MSC recruitment and proliferation within the conditioned media before culture with MSCs. Conditioned media was transferred into 1.5 mL tubes and stored at -20 deg C. Conditioned media from macrophage-T cell cocultures were then analyzed, and levels of IGF-1, WNT3A, TGFbeta 1 (R&D Systems), IL-10, CXCL12 (BioLegend), and WNT10B (Aviva Systems Biology, San Diego, CA) were measured by ELISA following manufacturer's instructions.

2.11. Effect of CD4 + or CD8 + T cell deficiency in de novo bone formation on titanium implants

To elucidate the role of CD4 + or CD8 + T cells in de novo bone formation, 12-week-old male WT, Cd4 -/ -, and Cd8 -/ -mice (n = 6) were anesthetized using isoflurane/O 2 gas and instrumented to access the medullary canal and smooth, rough, or rough-hydro Ti rods were implanted as described before. After 21 days, femurs were removed from mice following euthanasia and fixed in 10% formalin (ThermoFisher) for subsequent microCT analysis.
2.12. MicroCT analysis

The distal femoral metaphysis was scanned at a resolution of 4032 x 2688 pixels (voxel size of 4.00um) over 180 deg using a highresolution aluminum-copper filter, scanning energies of 80kV and 60 uA, 3940 ms exposure time. An image was acquired every 0.5 deg and frame averaging was conducted for every five frames using SkyScan 1276 (Bruker, Billerica, MA). Serial two-dimensional crosssections were assembled into 3-D reconstructions and analyzed using SkyScan software and CTAn (Bruker). For bone-to-implant contact analysis, a tubular region of interest (ROI) with a length of 3.75mm, an outer diameter of 1.12mm, and an inner diameter of 1.00mm, creating a volumetric sleeve aligned with the centroid of the implant was generated. For bone formation, ROIs of lengths of 3.75mm, with an inner diameter of 1.12 mm and an outer diameter of 1.35mm, were generated, creating a volumetric washer. All images were individually evaluated, and the ROI mask was applied to each image. A threshold of 10 0 0.0 0-250 0.0 0 Hounsfield units in the ROI was used to identify bone. Morphometric indices of bone volume selected for representation from the binarized volume of interest (VOI) included the bone volume/tissue volume (BV/TV) ratio, which quantifies the bone amount relative to the VOI size. 3D representations of bone formation were modeled using Dragonfly (Comet Technologies Canada Inc., Montral, QC).
2.13. Statistical analysis

Data are presented as mean +/- SD. Statistical analysis was performed using Prism9 (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA). Data were first subjected to the Shapiro-Wilk normality test. Results from this test indicate that the data were normally distributed. A one-factor, equal analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to test the null hypothesis that group means were equal at a significance level of alpha = 0.05, with post-hoc Tukey's HSD test for multiple comparisons.
3. Results
3.1. Titanium disks and implant characterization

Increased surface roughness and topography on rough and rough hydrophilic Ti in comparison to smooth Ti was seen by SEM ( Fig. 1 A). Higher roughness for rough (Sa = 3.28 u m) and rough hydrophilic Ti (Sa = 3.30 u m) was shown by confocal microscopy when compared to smooth Ti (Sa = 0.60 u m). Rough and rough hydrophilic Ti surfaces did not demonstrate quantitative or qualitative differences in surface roughness. Water contact angle measurements ( Fig. 1 B) showed that rough Ti ( theta = 91 deg ) was the most hydrophobic and rough-hydrophilic Ti ( theta = 0 deg ) was the most hydrophilic. Smooth Ti was normal ( theta = 63 deg ) compared to rough and rough hydrophilic Ti. The carbon content of the oxide layer was correlated with the wettability of our Ti surfaces. Rough hydrophilic Ti had the lowest carbon content in the oxide layer, while rough Ti had the highest carbon content in the oxide layer, demonstrated by surface chemical composition ( Fig. 1 C).

3.5. Deficiency of CD4 + or CD8 + T cells alters peri-implant cytokine profile in response to modified Ti in vivo

We identified production of pro-inflammatory (IL-2, IL-6, IL9, IL-17A, IL-17F, TNFalpha , IFNgamma ) and anti-inflammatory (IL-4, IL5, IL-10, IL-13, IL-22) cytokines in peri-implant tissue. At postoperative day 3, levels of pro-inflammatory IL-9 and IL-17A and anti-inflammatory IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13 were significantly lower in Cd4 -/ -mice ( Fig. 7 ). IL-2 and TNFalpha production was higher in Cd4 -/ -mice when compared to WT mice, specifically in response to smooth and rough Ti implants. IL-22 production was the highest in WT mice but did not show significant differences between Cd4 -/ -and Cd8 -/ -mice. Production of IL-6, IL-10, IL-17F, and IFNgamma did not demonstrate significant differences between Cd4 -/ -, Cd8 -/ -, and WT mice.
At 7 days post-implantation, production of pro-inflammatory IL2, IL-9, and IL-17A, as well as anti-inflammatory IL-4, IL-5, and IL-

13, were significantly lower in Cd4 -/ -mice when compared against WT mice. Levels of IFNgamma , IL-6, IL-10, and IL-22 did not show significant differences between Cd4 -/ -, Cd8 -/ -, and WT mice. TNFalpha production was the highest in response to smooth Ti implants, specifically in Cd4 -/ -mice.

On day 14 post-implantation, circulating levels of proinflammatory IL-6 and IFNgamma , as well as anti-inflammatory IL-4 and IL-10, were higher in Cd4 -/ -and Cd8 -/ -mice compared to WT mice. Production of IL-4 was the lowest in response to rough hydrophilic Ti implants in both WT and Cd8 -/ -mice, although IL-4 levels were the highest in response to these substrates in Cd4 -/ -mice. IL-5 levels were significantly lower in Cd4 -/ -mice. Production of other inflammatory cytokines did not show significant differences between Cd4 -/ -, Cd8 -/ -, and WT mice.
3.6. CD4 + or CD8 + T cells enhance bone formation and bone implant contact in response to modified Ti implants

We analyzed bone-to-implant contact and identified that WT mice produced significantly more bone around all modified Ti implants than Cd4 -/ -and Cd8 -/ -mice ( Fig. 8 A and 8 B). Cd4 -/ -mice showed the lowest peri-implant bone in response to smooth Ti implants, while the WT mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants demonstrated the highest bone-implant contact. We also quanti- fied new bone formation by analyzing bone volume as a proportion of total volume, and similarly, WT mice showed more new bone formation in response to all modified Ti implants when compared to Cd4 -/ -and Cd8 -/ -mice (Supplemental Fig. 1). Cd4 -/ -and Cd8 -/ -mice receiving rough or rough hydrophilic Ti implants produced more new bone than those receiving smooth Ti implants. Cd4 -/ -mice receiving rough hydrophilic implants showed lower bone formation than Cd8 -/ -mice and WT mice.

4. Discussion

We have recently shown that both innate and adaptive immune cells are altered by the physicochemical cues on biomaterial implants [ 8 , 15 ]. We have previously investigated the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, including the modifications in this study [ 1 , 8-11 , 15 ]. Rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants are utilized clinically due to their ability to attenuate the pro-inflammatory microenvironment and improve the formation of new bone in vivo when compared to smooth implants, promoting healing and implant success [ 1 , 3 ]. Varying the roughness of Ti implants provides two distinct inflammatory phenotypes of the immune response when examining the response to smooth and rough implants. Rough Ti implants upregulate the polarization of anti-inflammatory macrophages, production of antiinflammatory cytokines, and differentiation of MSCs to osteoblasts, further enhanced by increasing wettability [ 1 , 31-33 ]. Our group has demonstrated that surface roughness and wettability on Ti implants significantly affect T cell polarization. T cells significantly contribute to the inflammatory response and new bone formation post-implantation. Previously, we have demonstrated that a deficiency of alphabeta T cells results in a higher presence of neutrophils and pro-inflammatory macrophages at the implantation site. This study aimed to deconvolute the individual significance of CD4 + and CD8 + T cells, subsets of the alphabeta T cell family, in the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants. Our study identified that CD4 + and CD8 + T cells play different roles in the inflammatory response, and deficiency of either subset significantly impacts the behavior of innate immune cells, recruitment and proliferation of MSCs, and new bone formation around Ti implants.

Neutrophils are the first and most abundant immune cells recruited to the injury site by DAMPs after implantation [ 13 , 34 , 35 ]. Previously, we have demonstrated that neutrophil recruitment at the site of implantation is the highest at early time points (days 1 and 3), and proportions are reduced at later time points (days 7 and 14) [ 13 , 15 , 36 ]. However, mice with deficiency in alphabeta T cells have shown significantly elevated neutrophil populations at all time points analyzed, suggesting that alphabeta T cells are essential for the suppression and clearance of neutrophils in the inflammatory microenvironment [15] . Neutrophil survival is mediated by death receptors expressed on the cell membrane, and apoptosis of neutrophils can be induced by T cell secretion of TNFalpha [37] . Our study showed a lower proportion of neutrophils in mice lacking CD4 + or CD8 + T cells at early time points and an increased proportion at later time points compared to T cell-competent mice. These results suggest that CD4 + and CD8 + T cells play a significant role in neutrophil behavior by recruiting additional neutrophils early and resolving the inflammatory response later.

Fig. 8. Bone implant contact is significantly lower in mice deficient in CD4 + or CD8 + T cells. Analysis of bone-implant contact around modified Ti implants from WT, Cd4 -/ -, or Cd8 -/ -mice at 21 days post-implantation (n = 6). p < 0.05: A vs. WT, B vs. Cd4 -/ -, # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti.

Neutrophils have previously been shown to be modulated by CD4 + and CD8 + T cells via secretion of inflammatory cytokines [37-40] . IL-8 and IL-17A production by human CD4 + T cells has been shown to promote neutrophil recruitment in an in vitro coculture model [39] . IL-17A has been shown to play a vital role in regulating neutrophil behavior in several tissues, including lung, kidney, brain, spleen, gut, and liver, as mice deficient in interleukin 17 receptor (IL-17R) have shown significantly reduced recruitment of neutrophils in various immune responses [ 37 , 39 , 41 ]. Our data shows lower levels of IL-17A in Cd4 -/ -mice compared to T cell-competent mice, demonstrating that CD4 + T cells, specifically Th17, are the primary source of IL-17A after biomaterial implantation [42-44] . The role of IL-17A may be tissue-dependent, as a prior study involving murine skin wound healing showed that IL-17A deficiency also resulted in decreased neutrophil recruitment but accelerated wound healing in this model [45] . Additionally, secretion of IFNgamma and TNFalpha by CD8 + T cells has been shown to promote higher activation and viability of neutrophils compared to CD4 + T cells in a neutrophil-T cell co-culture model [ 40 , 46 ]. Here, we observed increased levels of TNFalpha in CD4 -/ -mice in all time points measured, coinciding with persistently elevated populations of neutrophils even at 14 days post-implantation, suggesting that CD4 + T cells are essential for immunomodulating the local environment. CD4 + Tregs have shown a suppressive effect on neutrophils in a prior in vitro study analyzing neutrophil response to lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-activated Tregs. Tregs promote neutrophil apoptosis via direct cell-cell contact and production of IL-10 and TGFbeta , as well as lower neutrophil secretion of reactive oxygen species and inflammatory cytokines [ 47 , 48 ]. Populations of neutrophils were most significantly impacted by the deficiency of CD4 + T cells. This suggests that CD4 + T cells play an important role in neutrophil recruitment and survival in the inflammatory response.

Macrophages play a significant role in immune cell crosstalk with T cells, as our group has previously shown that the presence of T cells can significantly increase the inflammatory polarization of macrophages in vitro [ 15 , 36 ]. T cells do not directly interact with the implant surface; therefore, T cell response to implantation is mediated by T cell interaction with the adherent cells on the biomaterial substrate [ 17 , 49 , 50 ]. This study demonstrates the impact of CD4 + or CD8 + T cell deficiency on the macrophage response to Ti implantation. Here, we show that CD4 + T cells are critical in controlling macrophage polarization, as mice deficient in CD4 + T cells have significantly higher pro-inflammatory and lower antiinflammatory macrophages both in vitro and in vivo. While our results show proand anti-inflammatory macrophages as a proportion of total macrophages, given the deficiency of either CD4 + or CD8 + T cells, we also quantified proand anti-inflammatory macrophages as a proportion of all immune cells (CD45 + ), which demonstrated fewer differences between Cd4 -/ -and Cd8 -/ -mice (Supplemental Fig. 2). CD4 + T cells produce several factors that promote pro-inflammatory macrophage activation, such as MIP1 alpha , MIP-1 beta , and ATAC [51] . IFNgamma production by T cells has been shown to increase the proliferation of macrophages and nitric oxide production. Additionally, Th2 cells and CD4 + Tregs appear to play a vital role in suppressing pro-inflammatory macrophages and activating anti-inflammatory macrophages via secretion of IL-4 and IL-10 [ 8 , 52-56 ]. Here, we show decreased production of IL-4 in CD4 + T cell-deficient mice, along with lower populations of antiinflammatory macrophages, demonstrating that CD4 + T cells are a significant source of this anti-inflammatory cytokine in the inflammatory response that contributes to macrophage activity. We also found diminished secretion of IL-9, a cytokine produced primarily by Th9 cells, in CD4 + T cell-deficient mice. Macrophage polarization has been shown to be impacted by IL-9 in a human in vitro model, showing that recombinant IL-9 treatment reduced pro-inflammatory marker expression and promoted TGFbeta secretion [57] . This upregulation of TGFbeta has been shown to elicit an anti-inflammatory effect by reducing the oxidative burst ability of macrophages [57] . Additionally, a model of antigen-induced arthritis examined IL-9 deficiency and found that IL-9 is an important cytokine for the activation of Tregs, enhancing the suppressive capacity of these cells and promoting the resolution of the inflammatory response [58] . In our study, we believe that reduced production of IL-9 in CD4 + deficient mice impairs the resolution of the inflammatory response.

Fig. 9. Secreted cytokines, proteins, and growth factors within the conditioned media from macrophage-T cell coculture experiments are altered by the deficiency of CD4 + or CD8 + T cells in response to modified Ti. Protein analysis of the conditioned media from macrophages cultured with T cells from either WT, Cd4 -/ -, or Cd8 -/ -mice (n = 6). p < 0.05: A vs. WT, B vs. Cd4 -/ -, # vs. smooth Ti, $ vs. rough Ti.

While mice deficient in CD8 + T cells did not demonstrate drastic differences in macrophage populations compared to CD4 + deficient mice, these cells still impact the inflammatory polarization of macrophages. Our study shows that CD8 + deficient mice demonstrate higher proportions of pro-inflammatory macrophages and lower anti-inflammatory macrophages in vitro and in vivo. CD8 + T cell interactions with macrophages are poorly characterized compared to CD4 + T cells. A previous study involving cardiotoxininduced muscle injury found that adoptive transfer of CD8 + T cells increased macrophage recruitment into the injury site via increased expression of MCP-1 [59] . Additionally, in studies using a high-fat diet-induced obesity mouse model, CD8 + T cells have been shown to significantly contribute to tissue inflammation associated with obesity, recruiting and activating macrophages within adipose tissue [60] . CD8 + T cells have also been shown to produce other factors, such as MCP-3 and RANTES, which can impact the migration and differentiation of macrophages [60] . Furthermore, CD8 + T cells have demonstrated a suppressive function on osteoclast differentiation from macrophages, previously shown in a co-culture model where CD8 + T cells prevented the formation of multi-nucleated osteoclasts via secretion of soluble molecules such as osteoprotegerin [61] . On the other hand, CD4 + T cells showed the ability to activate and differentiate osteoclasts. Future studies should examine the role of CD8 + T cells in isolation, as we demonstrate that deficiency of these cells impacts the polarization of macrophages in the peri-implant microenvironment.

Recruitment of MSCs after the initial inflammatory response post-implantation is imperative to facilitate new bone formation and osseointegration. Previously, we have demonstrated that the deficiency of alphabeta T cells attenuates MSC recruitment, proliferation, and peri-implant bone contact at 14 days post-implantation [15] . Interestingly, our data shows that the lack of CD4 + or CD8 + T cells significantly reduces new bone formation and bone-implant contact at 21 days post-implantation, with bone-implant contact significantly lowered in Cd4 -/ -mice. We found reduced levels of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) and WNT10B in mice deficient in either CD4 + or CD8 + T cells, two secreted products that have previously shown essential roles in new bone formation [ 62 , 63 ]. IGF-1 is a hormone primarily produced in the liver, but both IGF1 and WNT10B have been previously reported to be secreted locally by T cells and have demonstrated important functions that modulate osteoblastogenesis [64-66] . Here, we observed the lowest production of IGF-1 and WNT10B in cells, specifically from the results of the CD4 + deficient group. Other studies have reported that CD4 + and CD8 + T cells partially or entirely inhibit bone regeneration by blocking the osteogenic differentiation of MSCs. Yet, these reports utilize nude mice deficient in gammadelta T cells, a CD3 + T cell subset shown to significantly affect bone regeneration in vitro and in vivo [67-72] . In our study, our mice have competent gammadelta T cells, and we show that recruitment of MSCs is attenuated both in vitro and in vivo in mice deficient in either CD4 + or CD8 + T cells. These differences are only apparent at day 7 post-implantation, where CD4 -/ -mice demonstrate significantly reduced recruitment of MSCs, whereas at day 14 post-implantation, differences become less pronounced. CD4 + and CD8 + T cells have previously shown a role in MSC recruitment via secretion of soluble factors involved in MSC chemotaxis to the injury site [ 51 , 73-76 ]. Differences in bone formation may result from adherent progenitor cells differentiating on the implant surface that would be modulated by either CD4 + and CD8 + T cells or their effector subsets. We have shown that MSCs cultured on rough or rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces undergo osteogenic differentiation via integrin signaling. This creates an osteogenic microenvironment that increases osteogenic differentiation on cells not in contact with the biomaterial surface [7780] .
1. Introduction

The immune system plays an integral role in coordinating the inflammatory response and healing outcomes. My lab has demonstrated that macrophages orchestrate the interplay between innate immunity, adaptive immunity, and mesenchymal stem cell recruitment [1]. However, as we age, cells  lose  function  and  become  senescent,  including  macrophages.  Understanding  age-related immune  dysfunction  represents  an  essential  step  toward  improving  surgical  outcomes  in  our rapidly aging population, particularly as poor healing outcomes  after implant surgeries disproportionately affect older patients.

Intellectual Merit: The normal inflammatory response progresses through an initial inflammatory stage that clears debris and  pathogens,  followed  by  an  anti-inflammatory  stage that is immunomodulatory  and  pro-healing.  The  timing  between  these  stages  is  critical  for  tissue regeneration [1]. Recent work from our lab demonstrated that after titanium implant placement in older  animals,  we  observe  prolonged  inflammation,  increased  pro-inflammatory  cytokine production,  and  lower-quality  healing.  In  young  mice,  expression  of  inflammatory  cytokines elevated immediately post-surgery but returned to baseline by 21 days. In aged mice, however, expression remained elevated past 21 days, resulting in chronic inflammation and poor healing. Macrophages were among the most impacted cell types, which is significant given their central role in mediating pro- and anti-inflammatory responses.

My research objective is to investigate biomaterial-induced senescence in immune cells and use cellular rejuvenation methods to eliminate the senescent phenotype. I hypothesize that rejuvenation of senescent macrophages will accelerate resolution of the pro-inflammatory phase, reduce chronic inflammation, and improve wound healing in aged populations. While building on my advisor's expertise in macrophage biology [1,9], my project uniquely contributes by studying a novel therapeutic approach, as no prior studies have attempted to rejuvenate immune cells for applications in aging-related healing deficits.

Year 1 Aim 1 -Compare chemical reprogramming versus OSKM-mediated refunctionalization  of  macrophages  across  age  groups: Chemical  reprogramming  offers significant advantages over genetic methods for cellular rejuvenation. Chemical cocktails achieve more uniform distribution in culture media compared to plasmid transfection, which suffers from variable  uptake  efficiency  [2].  The  VC6TF  cocktail  (valproic  acid,  CHIR-99021,  E-616452, tranylcypromine,  and  forskolin)  achieves  rejuvenation  through  direct  epigenetic  modulation, including  demethylation  and  histone  acetylation  changes  [3,5],  and  demonstrates  rejuvenation within 4 days compared to 10-14 days for OSKM reprogramming [3,4]. I hypothesize that the VC6TF  chemical  cocktail  will  outperform  OSKM  plasmid  transfection  at  macrophage refunctionalization because the chemicals specifically demethylate chromatin  marks associated  with  senescence  [5]  and  distribute  evenly  throughout  culture  media,  unlike plasmid transfection which has inherently low and variable efficiency in primary immune cells [2]. Additionally, individual components of the VC6TF cocktail exhibit anti-inflammatory properties.  Valproic  acid  downregulates  pro-inflammatory  cytokines  (TNFalpha,  IL -1beta,  IL -6)  in macrophages  [6],  while  TGFbeta  pathway  modulation  by  E -616452  can  reduce  inflammatory responses [7]. If chemical refunctionalization cannot be achieved, chemical treatment should still  reduce  inflammatory  markers  in  aged  macrophages,  providing  therapeutic  benefit through adoptive transfer of cells with reduced inflammatory potential.

Macrophages will be isolated from bone marrow of mice in three age groups: young (3 months), middle-aged (10 months), and old (20 months). Experimental groups at each age will include: (1) untreated controls, (2) OSKM plasmid transfection with doxycycline induction, and (3) VC6TF chemical cocktail added to media. All cells will be cultured for 4, 7, and 10 days. For chemical treatments, cells will receive VC6TF at established concentrations [3]: valproic acid (0.5 mM), CHIR99021 (10 uM), E -616452 (10 uM), tranylcypromine (10 uM), and forskolin (10 uM). For OSKM  treatment,  cells  will  be  transfected  with  plasmids  containing  OSKM  and  rtTA  with doxycycline (2 mcg/mL) added to induce expression.

Refunctionalization will be assessed by: (1) senescence marker reduction (p16INK4A, senescenceassociated beta-galactosidase expression measured by flow cytometry and immunofluorescence, with success defined as >=50% reduction), (2) macrophage identity main tenance (CD45+/CD11b+/MHCII+/F4/80+/Mac+ by flow cytometry [1]), and (3) functional restoration (phagocytosis  assays).  Inflammatory  potential  will  be  measured  through  cytokine  secretion profiles by ELISA for TNFalpha, IL -1beta, IL -6, IL-10, TGFbeta.

Year 2 Aim 2 - Evaluate therapeutic efficacy of refunctionalized macrophages in aged mice with  titanium  implants: My  lab  previously  demonstrated  that  adoptive  transfer  of  antiinflammatory  macrophages  reduces  pro-inflammatory  responses  and  results  in  improved  bone regeneration [9]. I will use these techniques to evaluate whether refunctionalized macrophages resolve chronic inflammation and improve healing at implant sites. MaFIA mice (old, middle-aged, and young) will be injected with AP20187 for 3 days pre-implantation to deplete endogenous macrophages to <10% baseline. Mice will receive titanium implant surgery followed by immediate macrophage adoptive transfer through tail-vein injection. Experimental groups will repeat for all mouse age groups as: (1) transfer of aged untreated macrophages, (2) transfer of refunctionalized macrophages, and (3) transfer of young untreated macrophages. Mice will be sacrificed at 7, 14, and 28 days post-implantation.

Immune cell populations will be quantified by flow cytometry using markers for total macrophages (CD45+/CD11b+/MHCII+/Cd11c-/F4/80+), M1 macrophages (CD206-/CD80+), and M2 macrophages (CD80-/CD206+). Serum cytokines (TNFalpha,  IL -1beta,  IL -6,  IL-10,  TGFbeta)  will  be measured by ELISA. Bone formation will be assessed by micro-CT for Bone/Tissue volume and bone mineral density. I expect refunctionalized macrophage transfer to reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines,  accelerate  M2  polarization,  and  result  in  significantly  increased  BV/TV  at  day  28 compared to aged controls.

Broader Impacts: This research addresses a critical gap in our understanding of aging biology and  has  direct  implications  for  improving  healthcare  outcomes  in  older  adults.  By  targeting immune dysfunction at the cellular level, this work could lead to therapies that improve healing after surgeries. This fellowship will enable me to develop expertise in cellular reprogramming, chemical biology, advanced flow cytometry, and adoptive cell transfer essential for my career goal of  becoming an independent researcher in regenerative medicine and aging biology. I am  also currently committed to broadening participation in STEM  through actively mentoring underrepresented groups through local after-school programs such as Higher Achievement, where I encourage exploration in sciences through student engagement in planned programs that develop skills and passion for learning.

Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

Obesity has been shown to significantly alter physiological processes, including the behavior of immune cells, inducing a state of systemic chronic inflammation. Our study demonstrates that obesity-induced via a high-fat diet alters immune cell response to implanted biomaterials, with increased pro-inflammatory response and attenuated immunomodulation resulting in decreased biomaterial integration.

1. Introduction

Obesity is a metabolic disease that is the result of excessive body fat accumulation, negatively impacting health by increasing the risk of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and osteoarthritis [1,2]. Obesity is a global epidemic with an estimated prevalence of 40% among the world population, and it has been linked to the development of chronic inflammation [2]. Likewise, pro-inflammatory cytokine production is increased in animal models of high-fat diet (HFD)-induced obesity [3,4]. Chronic inflammation due to obesity plays a vital role in altering the function of adipose tissue, contributing to systemic insulin resistance and increasing immune cell infiltration of adipose tissue [1,5,6]. Neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells are among the immune cells previously shown to infiltrate the adipose tissue and promote systemic inflammation [6-10]. Macrophages within the adipose tissue in HFD-induced obesity models have been suggested to be the primary source of inflammatory factors in obesity, demonstrating increased polarization towards a pro-inflammatory phenotype [11-13]. Diet-induced obesity impairs T cell response in mice, with higher numbers of Th17 cells and reduced Treg cells in lymph nodes and spleen compared to a standard, normal-fat diet [14-16].

Metallic implants used for craniofacial and orthopedic applications are modified at the surface level to promote bone formation, also known as osseointegration [17-19]. The success of biomaterial implantation depends upon the recruitment of immune cells and the resolution of the inflammatory response in the peri-implant microenvironment that results in biomaterial-tissue integration [20-23]. Post-implantation, damage to the tissue causes the release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), initiating wound healing by recruiting immune cells to the implantation site. Innate immune cells, such as neutrophils and macrophages, are among the first immune cells recruited to the injury site, followed by T cells, as part of the adaptive response.

Neutrophils and macrophages are myeloid-derived cells that clear wound debris via phagocytosis and initiate the immune response [24,25]. Neutrophils release inflammatory mediators like myeloperoxidase (MPO), neutrophil elastase, and neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). Macrophages differentiate into pro- or anti-inflammatory macrophages depending on the biochemical and physical cues in the inflammatory microenvironment [25,26]. Our group has demonstrated that macrophage polarization is required to facilitate the amplification and resolution of the inflammatory response to implanted metallic biomaterials and orchestrate the recruitment of T cells and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) using titanium (Ti) as a model [22,25]. Ablating macrophages during the inflammatory response to Ti implants significantly attenuates T cell and MSC recruitment, compromising tissue regeneration [22].

T cells are immune cells found in lymphoid organs, recruited to the implantation site by chemokines and cytokines produced by neutrophils and macrophages at the biomaterialtissue interface [21,23,25,27]. We have shown that T cells are polarized at the implantation site via direct interaction with macrophages and cytokines in the peri-implant environment [23,25,27]. CD4+ T cells or T helper cells play an essential role in amplifying the immune response through interaction with antigen-presenting cells (i.e., macrophages and dendritic cells) and by controlling the inflammatory microenvironment [27]. CD4 +  T cells can polarize into specific subsets, including Th1, Th2, Th17, and regulatory T (Treg) cells, dependent upon circulating cytokines in the inflammatory microenvironment [21,27,28]. We have previously shown that T cells are required to resolve the inflammatory response, recruit MSCs, and promote bone formation in response to biomaterial implantation [21,27]. While our prior findings were observed in animals of average body weight and composition, the significance of obesity in the immune response and bone formation remains understudied. Earlier studies on obesity and the foreign body response to implantation have found that obesity can attenuate the healing response, resulting in poor osseointegration [29,30].

This study aimed to elucidate the role of obesity in the immune cell response to biomaterial implantation and its impact on bone formation. We hypothesized that obesity promotes a pro-inflammatory microenvironment that increases the inflammatory response of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, reducing bone formation.
2. Materials and Methods
2.1 Ti Disks, Implants, and Material Characterization

Rough and rough-hydrophilic titanium (Ti) disks and implants were provided by Institut Straumann AG (Basel, Switzerland), as described previously [21,31,32].15 mm disks were fabricated from 1 mm thick unalloyed titanium grade 2. Disks were degreased in an acetone bath and treated in a 2% ammonium fluoride, 2% hydrofluoric acid, and 10% nitric acid solution to create the smooth Ti pretreatment. Rough Ti disks were fabricated by sand-blasting the pretreated smooth Ti with 0.25-0.50 mm coarse-grit corundum and acid etching in a HCl and H 2 SO 4  solution. After sand-blasting and acid etching, rough Ti disks were rinsed with deionized water and dried. Rough-hydrophilic Ti disks were produced following the same protocol to fabricate rough Ti disks but, to prevent air exposure, rinsed under a nitrogen atmosphere and sealed in an isotonic NaCl solution. 4 mm Ti implants were produced from 1 mm diameter rods and modified using the same procedure as disks. All materials were sterilized by gamma -irradiation before use.

Scanning electron microscopy was used to qualitatively assess surface roughness (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany), and confocal microscopy was used for quantitative assessment (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um (n=3). A moving average Gaussian filter with a 30 um cut-off wavelength was used to calculate the arithmetic mean height of the scale-limited surface (Sa). Three samples were used for each surface type to calculate the mean surface roughness at six different areas. A Ram-Hart goniometer was used to measure the sessile drop contact angle to determine surface hydrophilicity (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 uL drops of deionized water were used to measure surface hydrophilicity at six locations per sample (n=3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PhI5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) to determine the oxide layer composition. A focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um was used to acquire spectra, utilizing a base pressure of 1x10 -7  Pa. FAT analyzer mode was used to run the instrument. Detail spectra of 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV were used for the pass energy for survey scans. CasaXPS was utilized for data analysis. Shirley background subtraction was used to integrate the signals. Measurements were taken on each sample in three separate areas.

2.2 HFD-induced Obesity Murine Model

20-week-old male C57BL/6J mice (The Jackson Laboratory, Stock #000664) were fed a diet containing 45 kcal% fat (D12451, Research Diets Inc., New Brunswick, NJ) or a standard diet containing 17 kcal% fat (Teklad LM-485, Inotiv, Madison, WI) for 6 weeks. Mice fed a 45 kcal% fat diet will be referred to as obese, and mice fed the standard diet will be referred to as lean. 26-week-old obese and lean mice were used for our experiments. The average weight of 26-week-old obese mice was 38.2 grams (standard deviation of 3.37 grams) compared to 22.6 grams (standard deviation of 1.66 grams) for lean mice. In this diet-induced obesity model, mice become pre-diabetic (Type 2) after eight weeks and diabetic after 16 weeks of a 45 kcal% fat diet [33]
2.3 Femoral Implantation Procedure

Isoflurane in O 2  gas was administered to mice to induce anesthesia, and mice were monitored for unconsciousness by pedal reflex. 1mg/kg buprenorphine ER-LAB (ZooPharm, Laramie, WY) was administered to mice before the surgical procedure to relieve postoperative pain for 72 hours. A scalpel was utilized to bisect the patellar tendon longitudinally and open the skin, overlaying the knee, until the femoral condyles were revealed. The medullary canal was accessed using a surgical dental bur, and a cylindrical rough or rough-hydrophilic Ti implant (=1mm, 4mm length) was then press fit (n=6 mice per implant condition). A digital X-ray system (NOMAD Pro 2, Dexis, Quakertown, PA) was used to confirm implant placement. Animals were monitored until initial ambulation and continually monitored every 24 hours. All animals were provided food and water ad libitum for the duration of the study. No signs of infection were present in this study. Animal procedures were used following a protocol approved by the Virginia Commonwealth University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (Protocol: AD10001108).
2.4 Impact of Obesity on Immune Response to Femoral Implants In Vivo

To determine the effect of obesity on the immune response to femoral implants, obese or lean mice were instrumented with femoral implants (n=6 mice per condition). Mice were euthanized on postoperative days 3 and 7 for cytokine and flow cytometry analysis and on day 21 for micro-computed tomography (microCT). Blood was collected via cardiac puncture and stored in a 1.5 mL tube. Blood was centrifuged at 500 g for 10 minutes, and plasma was isolated and stored at -80degC for analysis. Femurs were collected by blunt dissection. For peri-implant cell analysis, femurs were cut mid-shaft, and the implant and surrounding marrow were flushed using Accutase (Innovative Cell Technologies, San Diego, CA). For microCT analysis, femurs were fixed in a 10% formalin solution (ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA).
2.5 Flow Cytometry

Flow cytometry was conducted from bone marrow single-cell suspensions. ACK lysing buffer (ThermoFisher Scientific) was used to lyse red blood cells prior to incubation with antibodies. TruStain FcX (anti-CD16/32, BioLegend, San Diego, CA) was used to block Fc receptors. Antibodies specific for surface markers were used to identify neutrophils, total macrophages, pro-inflammatory macrophages, anti-inflammatory macrophages, MSCs, CD4 +  T cells, CD8 +  T cells, Th1, Th2, Th17, and Tregs (BioLegend, Supplemental Table 1). The gating strategy was established using single-color controls and fluorescent minus one, as previously described [27]. The samples were analyzed using a Cytek Aurora CS Cytometer (Cytek Biosciences, Fremont, CA). A minimum of 200,000 events were collected per sample, and FlowJo software (FlowJo LLC, Ashland, OR) was used for sample analysis.
2.6 Analysis of Adipokines and Cytokines in Serum

Levels of adiponectin, C-reactive protein (CRP), leptin, and resistin were measured in collected plasma by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA, RayBio, Peachtree Corners, GA) following the manufacturer's instructions. Circulating IL-1 beta , IL-4, IL-6, and TNFalpha levels were measured by ELISA (BioLegend) following the manufacturer's instructions.

2.7 MicroCT Analysis

To understand how obesity impacts bone formation after biomaterial implantation, a resolution of 4032 x 2688 pixels (voxel size of 7.00um) was used to scan the distal femoral metaphysis over 180deg. A 0.5mm high-resolution aluminum filter with an exposure time of 630 ms and 60kV and 200 uA scanning energies was used. SkyScan 1276 (Bruker, Billerica, MA) was used every 0.15deg to capture an image. SkyScan software and CTAn (Bruker) were used for the assembly and analysis of 3-D reconstructions from serial two-dimensional cross-sections. A tubular region of interest (ROI) was utilized in the distal portion of each femur to quantify de-novo bone formation. A volumetric sleeve aligned with the centroid of the implant was used to create the ROI, encompassing a length of 3.75 mm, an outer diameter of 1.12mm, and an inner diameter of 1.00mm. The ROI mask was applied to each image individually to evaluate all images. Bone was identified using a threshold of 1000.002500.00 Hounsfield units in the ROI. Dragonfly (Object Research Systems, Montral, QC)

was used to create 3D representations of bone formation. Mineral density analysis was performed after calibration using bone calibration phantoms with different hydroxyapatite (HA) densities (0, 100, 200, 400, 800 mg HA/cm 3 ) and density reported as tissue mineral density (TMD) in mg HA/cm 3 .
2.8 Macrophage Isolation

26-week-old C57BL/6J obese or lean mice were used to isolate and differentiate bone marrow-derived murine macrophages as described previously [21,27]. After flushing bone marrow from isolated femurs with PBS and treatment with ACK lysing buffer, RPMI 1640 (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 mcg/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), and 30 ng/mL macrophage colony-stimulating factor (M-CSF, BioLegend) was used to derive nave macrophages. Cells were cultured at 37degC, 5% CO2, and 100% humidity. Fresh cell culture medium was supplemented on day 4 of culture. After 7 days, macrophages were detached with Accutase for use in subsequent experiments.
2.9 T Cell Isolation

26-week-old male C57BL/6J obese or lean mice were used to isolate and differentiate spleen-derived T cells, as previously described [21,27]. After harvest, spleens were homogenized, and ACK lysing buffer was used to remove red blood cells. Splenocytes were plated at a cell seeding density of 1,000,000 cells/well in 6-well plates to derive nave T cells. Cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 Medium (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 mcg/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), 0.05 mM beta -mercaptoethanol (ThermoFisher Scientific), 25 uL of prewashed and resuspended Dynabeads Mouse T-Activator CD3/CD28 (ThermoFisher Scientific), and 30 U/mL recombinant mouse IL-2 (BioLegend) for 3 days at 37degC, 5% CO2, and 100% humidity. Activated T cells were harvested after 3 days for use in subsequent experiments.
2.10 Effect of Obesity on Macrophage Response to Modified Ti in Vitro

To examine the effect of obesity on macrophage response to modified Ti, bone marrowderived macrophages from obese mice were seeded in 24-well plates on rough or roughhydrophilic Ti disks (n=6) at a density of 200,000 cells/well. Nave macrophages isolated from lean mice were seeded on rough or rough-hydrophilic Ti substrates as controls. Macrophages were cultured for 24 hours before media was removed, and macrophages were detached with Accutase (Innovative Cell Technologies) for flow cytometry analysis. Flow cytometry was conducted and analyzed as described in Section 2.5 to identify proinflammatory and anti-inflammatory macrophages. A minimum of 50,000 events were collected for each sample.
2.11 Impact of Obesity on T Cell Polarization In Vitro
Acta Biomater. 2025 January 15; 192: 473-486. doi:10.1016/j.actbio.2024.11.040.

To understand the impact of obesity on T cell polarization in response to macrophages cultured to modified Ti in vitro, bone marrow-derived macrophages isolated from either obese or lean mice were seeded in 24-well plates on either rough or rough-hydrophilic Ti disks (n=6) at a density of 200,000 cells/well. After 24 hours, activated T cells from obese-derived or lean-derived were added to the macrophage cultures at a seeding density of 300,000 cells/well. All cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 Medium (ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (ThermoFisher Scientific), 50 U/mL penicillin-50 mcg/mL streptomycin (ThermoFisher Scientific), and 0.05 mM beta -mercaptoethanol (ThermoFisher Scientific). Macrophages and T cells were co-cultured for 48 hours before T cells were isolated from the cell culture medium for flow cytometry analysis. T helper cell (Th1, Th2, Th17, Treg) subsets were analyzed using the flow cytometry methodology described above in section 2.5 and previously[27]. A minimum of 100,000 events were collected per sample for analysis.
2.12 Alteration of the Immune Cell Response by Adoptive Transfer of Bone Marrow from Obese Mice to Wild-Type Mice

To elucidate if the increased inflammatory phenotype observed in immune cells from obese mice persists in a normal environment, we performed an adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells from obese or lean mice into wild-type mice following Ti biomaterial implantation. Cells for adoptive transfer were isolated from the bone marrow of 26-week-old male C57BL/6J obese or lean mice. Mice were euthanized by CO 2  asphyxiation, and femur bones were removed. The bone marrow was flushed with PBS and treated with ACK buffer to remove erythrocytes (ThermoFisher Scientific). Cells were pooled and labeled with CellTracker TM  Green CMFDA Dye (ThermoFisher Scientific) for adoptive transfer experiments.

26-week-old C57BL/6J lean mice were instrumented with rough Ti implants (n=6/condition) as described above. Immediately following the surgical procedure, 1.5 x10 6  fluorescentlytagged bone marrow cells were injected in 100 uL of PBS via the tail vein. Mice were fed a standard diet ad libitum following surgery. Mice were euthanized on postoperative days 3 and 7 for cytokine/adipokine and flow cytometry analysis and on day 21 for microCT.
2.13 Statistical Analysis

Data are presented as mean +/- SD. Prism10 (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA) was used for statistical analysis. The Shapiro-Wilk normality test was first performed on data to determine if data was normally distributed. The null hypothesis that group means were equal at a significance level of alpha =0.05 was tested using one-way or two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). One-way ANOVA was utilized for baseline assessment of immune cells, adipokines, and cytokines between lean and obese mice prior to implantation. One-way ANOVA was also performed to compare the effect of adoptively transferring obese-derived or lean-derived bone marrow to analyze secretion of adipokines and cytokines, bone implant contact, and tissue mineral density. Two-way ANOVA was performed for in vitro and in vivo experiments involving rough or rough-hydrophilic Ti to compare the effect of both obesity and surface hydrophilicity. Multiple comparisons were conducted using post-hoc Tukey's HSD test.
3. Results
3.1 Titanium Disks and Implant Characterization

Rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces were similar in surface roughness, both quantitatively and qualitatively (Figure 1A). Rough-hydrophilic Ti ( theta = 0deg) was more hydrophilic, and rough Ti ( theta = 93deg) was more hydrophobic, as determined by analysis of the water contact angle (Figure 1B). The wettability of rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces was correlated with the quantity of carbon in the oxide layer. Rough Ti demonstrated a higher quantity of carbon in the oxide layer than rough-hydrophilic Ti.
3.2 Obesity Promotes Pro-Inflammatory Innate Immune Response within Peri-implant Microenvironment In Vivo
Obesity Prolongs the Pro-Inflammatory Response and Attenuates Bone Healing on Titanium Implants

At baseline prior to implantation, obese mice demonstrated higher proportions of neutrophils and pro-inflammatory macrophages compared to lean mice (Supplemental Figure 2). Total macrophages and anti-inflammatory macrophages were similar between lean and obese mice.

On day 3 post-implantation, lean mice receiving rough Ti implants showed higher populations of neutrophils, total macrophages, and pro-inflammatory macrophages than rough-hydrophilic Ti (Figure 2). Lean mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants showed higher populations of anti-inflammatory macrophages when compared to rough Ti. Obese mice demonstrate an altered innate immune response to rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti. On postoperative day 3, total macrophages and pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly higher in obese mice than in lean mice, while anti-inflammatory macrophages in obese mice were lower.

At 7 days post-implantation, lean mice receiving rough Ti implants demonstrated higher populations of neutrophils, total macrophages, and pro-inflammatory macrophages than mice implanted with rough-hydrophilic Ti (Figure 2). Obese mice had higher populations of neutrophils, total macrophages, and pro-inflammatory macrophages than lean mice. Flow cytometry scatter plots for neutrophils, total macrophages, and pro- and anti-inflammatory macrophages at day 7 post-implantation can be found in Supplementary Figure 4.
3.3 Obesity Alters Recruitment of T Cells and MSCs in the Peri-implant Microenvironment In Vivo

Next, we analyzed the effect of obesity on the recruitment of T cells and MSCs in response to modified Ti implantation. Prior to implantation, obese mice showed higher proportions of CD8 +  T cells and lower CD4 +  T cells when compared to lean mice (Supplemental Figure 2). At day 3 post-implantation, we did not note differences in populations of CD4 +  T cells, CD8 +  T cells, and MSCs between implant surfaces (Figure 3). When compared to lean mice, obese mice demonstrated lower populations of CD4 +  T cells and MSCs. Conversely, CD8 + T cells were significantly higher in obese mice compared to lean mice.

On postoperative day 7, mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants showed lower populations of CD8 +  T cells and higher recruitment of MSCs (Figure 3). CD4 +  T cells did not demonstrate differences between rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants. Obese mice demonstrated higher populations of CD4 +  and CD8 +  T cells when compared to lean mice. MSCs in obese mice were significantly lower than in lean mice. Flow cytometry scatter plots for T cells and MSCs at post-operative day 7 can be found in Supplementary Figure 5.
3.4 Obesity Modulates Helper T Cell Polarization in the Peri-implant Microenvironment In Vivo

We also were interested in how obesity affects the polarization of CD4 +  T cell subsets that contribute to inflammation in the peri-implant microenvironment. Pre-implantation, obese mice showed higher polarization of Th1 and CD4 +  Tregs than lean mice (Supplemental Figure 3). Populations of Th2 and Th17 were significantly lower in obese mice than in lean mice.

At 3 days post-implantation, mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants showed lower Th1 and Th17 cells and higher populations of Th2 and CD4 +  Tregs (Figure 4). Obese mice demonstrated significantly higher polarization of Th1 and Th17 subsets when compared to lean mice. Additionally, obese mice polarized less CD4 +  T cells to Th2 and Tregs. Interestingly, the polarization of Th1, Th17, and Treg subsets from obese mice was similar between mice receiving rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants.

On day 7 post-implantation, lean mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants showed lower polarization of Th1, Th2, Th17, and CD4 +  Tregs than rough Ti (Figure 4). Obese mice demonstrated significantly higher polarization to Th1 and Th17 subsets and reduced polarization of Th2 and Tregs compared to lean mice. Obese mice receiving either modified Ti implant showed similar polarization of Tregs. Flow cytometry scatter plots for CD4 +  T cell subsets at day 7 post-implantation can be found in Supplementary Figure 6.

3.5 Systemic Adipokine and Cytokine Production is Significantly Skewed by Obesity In Vivo

Next, we examined the impact of obesity on the systemic production of adipokines and cytokines. At baseline, serum levels of leptin, resistin, and CRP were significantly higher in obese mice than in lean mice (Figure 5). Production of adiponectin was significantly lower at baseline in obese mice. Additionally, serum levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-17A, and TNFalpha were significantly higher in obese mice.

On day 3 post-implantation, serum levels of adiponectin, CRP, and resistin in lean mice were similar between different modified Ti implants (Figure 6). Leptin was lower in lean mice receiving rough-hydrophilic implants compared to rough Ti. IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-17A, and TNFalpha levels were lower in mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants. Serum from obese mice had higher levels of CRP, leptin, resistin, IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-17A, and TNFalpha than that from lean mice (Figure 6). In obese mice, levels of these proteins were lower in mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants. Adiponectin was significantly lower in obese mice.

At day 7 post-implantation, CRP, leptin, and resistin did not show differences between lean mice receiving different implants (Figure 7). Mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants showed higher levels of adiponectin and lower levels of IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-17A, and TNFalpha . In obese mice, CRP, leptin, resistin, IL-1 beta , IL-6, IL-17A, and TNFalpha levels were significantly higher when compared to lean mice. Adiponectin was significantly lower in obese mice, although these levels did not differ between rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants.
3.6 Bone Formation is Significantly Attenuated by Obesity In Vivo

To understand if obesity affects bone formation, we analyzed bone formation at 21 days post-implantation (Figure 8). In lean mice, bone formation was higher on rough-hydrophilic Ti implants than on rough Ti. Obese mice produced significantly less bone around Ti implants than lean mice, and the effect of implant hydrophilicity was abrogated (Figure 8). Additionally, we analyzed tissue mineral density and found that tissue mineral density was higher in lean mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants when compared to rough Ti (Supplemental Figure 1A). Obese mice demonstrated significantly lower quantities of tissue mineral density when compared to lean mice.
3.7 Obesity Perturbs Helper T Cell Polarization in Macrophage-T Cell Co-cultures In Vitro

Next, we used a co-culture model to examine how obesity induces polarization of T cell subsets in vitro. We analyzed the impact of obese-derived macrophages on the polarization of lean-derived T cells and alterations in T cell polarization when obese-derived T cells were cultured with lean-derived macrophages. Polarization of Th1 and Th17 cells was lower in response to lean-derived macrophages cultured on rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces in comparison to rough Ti (Figure 9). Th2 and Treg polarization was the highest in response to lean-derived macrophages cultured on rough-hydrophilic Ti when compared against rough Ti.

Analyzing the effect of obesity on T cell polarization, Th1 and Th17 cells were higher in response to obese-derived macrophages compared to co-culture with lean-derived macrophages (Figure 9). Treg populations were lower in response to obese-derived macrophages when compared to Tregs cultured with lean-derived macrophages.

Examining the polarization of obese-derived T cells cultured with lean-derived macrophages, we found that Th1 cells were lower than co-cultures with obese-derived macrophages (Figure 9). Th17 cell polarization was dependent on surface hydrophilicity, but obese-derived Th17 cells were higher than co-cultures with lean-derived macrophages and T cells. The polarization of obese-derived Th2 and Treg cells was significantly attenuated compared to other groups with lean-derived T cells.
